topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT403 - MACHINE LEARNING II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Different Activation Functions: Sign Function, Sigmoid Function, Hyperbolic Tangent Function, ReLU Function, Leaky ReLU Function, Softmax Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Activation Function?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** An **Activation Function** in an artificial neural network is a mathematical function applied to the weighted sum of inputs (plus bias) at a neuron. It determines whether a particular neuron should be *activated* or not, by transforming the linear input signal into a non-linear output signal, thereby enabling the network to learn complex, non-linear mappings between input and output spaces.

Mathematically, for a neuron receiving inputs $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ with weights $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$ and bias $b$:

$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$

The activation function $f(\cdot)$ produces the neuron's output:

$$a = f(z)$$

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bouncer at a Club"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a neuron as a *doorman/bouncer* at an exclusive club. Inputs from friends arriving at the door carry varying degrees of "importance" (weights). The bouncer tallies a total "vibe score" $z$. Then the **activation function** acts as the bouncer's *decision rule*:
> - A **Sign function** says: "Either let them in (1) or don't (-1), no in-between."
> - A **Sigmoid** says: "Let them in *smoothly* between 0% and 100% based on vibe."
> - A **ReLU** says: "If vibe is positive, let them fully in; if negative, slam the door to zero."
>
> The choice of "bouncer rule" determines how the entire club (network) behaves!

### 1.2 Why Do We Need Activation Functions?

Without a non-linear activation function, a deep neural network collapses into a **single linear transformation**, no matter how many layers it has. The composition of linear functions is itself linear:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = W_2(W_1 \mathbf{x}) = (W_2 W_1) \mathbf{x} = W_{eff} \mathbf{x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** Non-linearity from activation functions is what gives neural networks their **Universal Approximation** capability — the theoretical ability to approximate *any* continuous function to arbitrary precision, given sufficient neurons.

### 1.3 Properties of a "Good" Activation Function

| Desired Property | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|
| **Non-linear** | Enables learning of complex patterns |
| **Differentiable** | Allows gradient-based backpropagation |
| **Monotonic** | Ensures stable, consistent error surfaces |
| **Computationally cheap** | Reduces training time for large networks |
| **Zero-centered output (preferred)** | Helps optimization converge faster |
| **Bounded range (often)** | Prevents exploding activations |

---

## 1.4 Overview of the Six Activation Functions

| # | Function | Output Range | Primary Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sign** | $\{-1, +1\}$ | Bipolar outputs, classic perceptron |
| 2 | **Sigmoid (Logistic)** | $(0, 1)$ | Binary classification output layer |
| 3 | **Tanh (Hyperbolic Tangent)** | $(-1, 1)$ | Hidden layers in shallow networks |
| 4 | **ReLU** | $[0, \infty)$ | Modern deep network hidden layers |
| 5 | **Leaky ReLU** | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | ReLU variant avoiding dead neurons |
| 6 | **Softmax** | $(0, 1)$, sum $= 1$ | Multi-class classification output layer |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sign (Step / Hard Threshold) Function

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The sign function squashes its input to one of two discrete values: $+1$ (positive) or $-1$ (negative), with $0$ mapping to $0$ in some variants.

$$f(z) = \text{sign}(z) = \begin{cases} +1, & z > 0 \\ 0, & z = 0 \\ -1, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

### Characteristics
- **Non-differentiable** at $z = 0$ and derivative is **0 almost everywhere** elsewhere → **cannot be used with gradient descent**.
- Produces only **bipolar binary outputs** — historically used in the original **Rosenblatt Perceptron (1958)**.
- Output range: $\{-1, +1\}$ (or $\{0, 1\}$ in the unipolar Heaviside variant).

### Limitations
> [!WARNING]
> The sign function is **not used in modern deep learning** because its zero-gradient everywhere prevents backpropagation from updating weights.

---

## 2.2 Sigmoid (Logistic) Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The sigmoid function is a smooth, S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve that maps any real-valued input to the open interval $(0, 1)$.

$$f(z) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

### Characteristics
- **Smooth and differentiable everywhere.**
- Output range: $(0, 1)$ — interpretable as a **probability**.
- **Monotonically increasing.**
- Suffers from the **Vanishing Gradient Problem** at the extremes ($z \to \pm\infty$, gradient $\to 0$).
- Output is **not zero-centered** → causes zig-zagging gradient updates during optimization.

### Derivative (a beautiful algebraic identity!)

$$f'(z) = \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)$$

### Real-World Utility
Used in the **output layer of binary classifiers** (e.g., logistic regression, spam detection, disease/no-disease classifiers) where the prediction must lie in $[0, 1]$.

---

## 2.3 Hyperbolic Tangent (tanh) Function

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The hyperbolic tangent is a rescaled and shifted version of the sigmoid, mapping inputs to the range $(-1, +1)$.

$$f(z) = \tanh(z) = \frac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$$

### Key Relationship to Sigmoid

$$\tanh(z) = 2\,\sigma(2z) - 1$$

### Characteristics
- **Zero-centered** output (range $(-1, 1)$) — preferred over sigmoid for hidden layers.
- Same **vanishing gradient** problem as sigmoid at extremes.
- Stronger gradients than sigmoid because of steeper derivative around $z=0$.

### Derivative

$$f'(z) = 1 - \tanh^2(z)$$

### Real-World Utility
Preferred in **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)** and **shallow feedforward networks** for hidden layers, especially when the data is naturally centered around zero (e.g., normalized pixel values in $[-1, 1]$).

---

## 2.4 ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** ReLU outputs the input directly if it is positive; otherwise, it outputs zero. It is the **default activation** for hidden layers in modern deep networks.

$$f(z) = \text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z) = \begin{cases} z, & z \ge 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

### Characteristics
- **Computationally extremely cheap** — just a thresholding operation.
- **Does not saturate in the positive region** (gradient = 1) → alleviates vanishing gradient for positive inputs.
- **Sparse activation** — about 50% of neurons output exactly 0 for any given input (efficient representation).
- **Not zero-centered.**
- Suffers from the **"Dying ReLU" problem** — once a neuron's weights push it into a regime where it always outputs 0, its gradient is also 0 and it never recovers.

### Derivative (sub-gradient form)

$$f'(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z > 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

(At $z = 0$, conventionally $f'(0) = 0$ or $1$; choice does not matter in practice.)

### Real-World Utility
Used in **CNNs (Convolutional Neural Networks)**, **deep MLPs**, **transformer feedforward blocks** — basically the workhorse of contemporary deep learning (Krizhevsky et al., 2012 AlexNet popularized it).

---

## 2.5 Leaky ReLU Function

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Leaky ReLU is a modification of ReLU that allows a small, non-zero gradient when the input is negative. The negative slope $\alpha$ is a small constant (typically $0.01$).

$$f(z) = \text{LReLU}(z) = \begin{cases} z, & z \ge 0 \\ \alpha z, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

Equivalently:

$$f(z) = \max(\alpha z,\, z), \quad \alpha \in (0, 1)$$

### Characteristics
- **Fixes the "Dying ReLU" problem** by ensuring negative inputs still produce a small gradient.
- All other benefits of ReLU are preserved.
- Slightly more expensive than ReLU (multiplication by $\alpha$ on the negative side).
- **Parametric ReLU (PReLU)** learns $\alpha$ from data; **Randomized ReLU (RReLU)** samples $\alpha$ randomly during training.

### Derivative

$$f'(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z > 0 \\ \alpha, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

### Real-World Utility
Used in **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)** and other deep architectures where dead neurons during adversarial training became a problem.

---

## 2.6 Softmax Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The softmax function (also called *normalized exponential*) converts a vector of $K$ real numbers into a probability distribution over $K$ classes, where each output lies in $(0, 1)$ and all outputs sum to $1$.

For an input vector $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_K)$:

$$f(z_i) = \text{softmax}(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}, \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, K$$

### Characteristics
- Output is a **proper probability distribution** — guarantees $\sum_i \sigma(z_i) = 1$.
- **Exponentiation** ensures outputs are strictly positive.
- **Differentiable** — Jacobian matrix used during backpropagation.
- Suffers from **numerical overflow** when $z_i$ is very large → handled by subtracting $\max_j z_j$ from all inputs (mathematically equivalent due to the shift-invariance of the ratio).
- **Generalizes the logistic sigmoid** to multi-class settings (sigmoid is softmax for $K=2$ with zero on the second logit).

### Derivative — The Jacobian Matrix

For the $i$-th output with respect to the $j$-th input:

$$\frac{\partial\, \text{softmax}(z_i)}{\partial z_j} = \text{softmax}(z_i)\bigl(\delta_{ij} - \text{softmax}(z_j)\bigr)$$

where $\delta_{ij}$ is the Kronecker delta.

When $i = j$ (diagonal entries):

$$\frac{\partial\, \text{softmax}(z_i)}{\partial z_i} = \text{softmax}(z_i)\bigl(1 - \text{softmax}(z_i)\bigr)$$

When $i \neq j$ (off-diagonal entries):

$$\frac{\partial\, \text{softmax}(z_i)}{\partial z_j} = -\text{softmax}(z_i)\,\text{softmax}(z_j)$$

### Real-World Utility
Used in the **output layer of multi-class classifiers** — image classification (ImageNet), language modeling, any task requiring mutually exclusive class probabilities.

---

## 2.7 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Function $f(z)$ | Output Range | Derivative $f'(z)$ | Differentiable? | Centered? |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $\text{sign}(z)$ | $\{-1, +1\}$ | $0$ a.e.; undefined at $0$ | ❌ No | No |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | $\sigma(z)\bigl(1-\sigma(z)\bigr)$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| 3 | $\dfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $1-\tanh^{2}(z)$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| 4 | $\max(0, z)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $\begin{cases}1 & z>0\\0 & z<0\end{cases}$ | ✅ Yes (sub-grad) | ❌ No |
| 5 | $\max(\alpha z, z)$, $\alpha \in (0,1)$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $\begin{cases}1 & z>0\\ \alpha & z<0\end{cases}$ | ✅ Yes (sub-grad) | ❌ No |
| 6 | $\dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | $(0, 1)$, $\sum = 1$ | $S_i(\delta_{ij} - S_j)$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |

### Decision Rules for Choosing an Activation Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize these rules — they appear as short-answer questions almost every semester.
> - **Binary classification output layer** → Sigmoid
> - **Multi-class classification output layer** → Softmax
> - **Regression output layer** → Linear (identity) — *not in this module's list, but worth knowing*
> - **Hidden layers of deep networks** → ReLU (default)
> - **When neurons are dying (ReLU)** → Leaky ReLU or PReLU
> - **RNN hidden states** → tanh (and sigmoid at gates)
> - **Classic Perceptron / bipolar outputs** → Sign

---

## 2.8 Side-by-Side Intuition Chart

| Function | Mental Model |
|---|---|
| **Sign** | A light switch — ON or OFF, no dimming. |
| **Sigmoid** | A dimmer switch — smoothly fades from dark (0) to bright (1). |
| **Tanh** | A bipolar dimmer — smoothly fades from "anti" (-1) through neutral to "pro" (+1). |
| **ReLU** | A one-way mirror — only positive light passes, negative light is blocked. |
| **Leaky ReLU** | A tinted one-way mirror — some negative light bleeds through (×0.01). |
| **Softmax** | A vote-counting machine — turns raw scores into percentages that sum to 100%. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📕 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sigmoid Function Derivative

We start from the sigmoid definition:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 1:** Rewrite using a negative exponent to make differentiation cleaner.

$$\sigma(z) = (1 + e^{-z})^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the chain rule $\frac{d}{dz}[u(z)]^{-1} = -[u(z)]^{-2} \cdot u'(z)$.

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = -(1 + e^{-z})^{-2} \cdot \frac{d}{dz}(1 + e^{-z})$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate the inner term.

$$\frac{d}{dz}(1 + e^{-z}) = -e^{-z}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back.

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = -(1 + e^{-z})^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-z}) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^{2}}$$

**Step 5:** Split the fraction cleverly — multiply and divide numerator and denominator by $(1 + e^{-z})$.

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^{2}} \cdot \frac{(1 + e^{-z})}{(1 + e^{-z})}$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the pieces.

- Numerator becomes: $\dfrac{e^{-z}(1 + e^{-z})}{(1 + e^{-z})^{2}} = \dfrac{e^{-z} + e^{-2z}}{1}$... Actually, the cleanest manipulation is:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 7:** Factor the second term as $1 - \sigma(z)$.

$$\frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}} = \frac{(1 + e^{-z}) - 1}{1 + e^{-z}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = 1 - \sigma(z)$$

**Step 8:** Combine to get the famous identity.

$$\boxed{\;\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This identity is computationally invaluable during backpropagation — we already have $\sigma(z)$ cached from the forward pass, so computing the gradient costs essentially zero extra forward computation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the tanh Function Derivative

Start from the definition:

$$\tanh(z) = \frac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 1:** Apply the quotient rule $\left(\frac{u}{v}\right)' = \frac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}$.

$$u = e^{z} - e^{-z}, \quad u' = e^{z} + e^{-z}$$

$$v = e^{z} + e^{-z}, \quad v' = e^{z} - e^{-z}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the quotient rule.

$$\tanh'(z) = \frac{(e^{z} + e^{-z})(e^{z} + e^{-z}) - (e^{z} - e^{-z})(e^{z} - e^{-z})}{(e^{z} + e^{-z})^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize each piece — the numerator is $(v^2 - u^2) = (v-u)(v+u)$.

$$v^2 - u^2 = (e^{z} + e^{-z})^{2} - (e^{z} - e^{-z})^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Expand using $(a+b)^2 - (a-b)^2 = 4ab$ with $a = e^{z}$, $b = e^{-z}$.

$$= 4 \cdot e^{z} \cdot e^{-z} = 4$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back.

$$\tanh'(z) = \frac{4}{(e^{z} + e^{-z})^{2}}$$

**Step 6:** Rewrite in terms of $\tanh(z)$. We know:

$$\tanh^{2}(z) = \left(\frac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}\right)^{2} = \frac{(e^{z} - e^{-z})^{2}}{(e^{z} + e^{-z})^{2}}$$

And

$$1 - \tanh^{2}(z) = \frac{(e^{z} + e^{-z})^{2} - (e^{z} - e^{-z})^{2}}{(e^{z} + e^{-z})^{2}} = \frac{4}{(e^{z} + e^{-z})^{2}}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(z)\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour at Critical Points

| Function $f(z)$ | Value at $z = -5$ | Value at $z = 0$ | Value at $z = +5$ | Max Gradient |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Sign** | $-1$ | $0$ | $+1$ | Undefined |
| **Sigmoid** | $\approx 0.0067$ | $0.5$ | $\approx 0.9933$ | $0.25$ (at $z=0$) |
| **Tanh** | $\approx -0.9999$ | $0$ | $\approx +0.9999$ | $1$ (at $z=0$) |
| **ReLU** | $0$ | $0$ | $5$ | $1$ (for $z>0$) |
| **Leaky ReLU** ($\alpha=0.01$) | $-0.05$ | $0$ | $5$ | $1$ (for $z>0$) |
| **Softmax** (depends on vector) | depends | depends | depends | $0.25$ (at uniform) |

### Worked Example: Softmax Numerical Evaluation

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** Compute softmax for $\mathbf{z} = (2.0,\, 1.0,\, 0.1)$ — a 3-class classifier output.

**Step 1:** Compute exponentials.

$$e^{z_1} = e^{2.0} = 7.389, \quad e^{z_2} = e^{1.0} = 2.718, \quad e^{z_3} = e^{0.1} = 1.105$$

**Step 2:** Sum them up.

$$\sum_{j=1}^{3} e^{z_j} = 7.389 + 2.718 + 1.105 = 11.212$$

**Step 3:** Compute the softmax outputs.

$$\text{softmax}(z_1) = \frac{7.389}{11.212} \approx 0.659$$

$$\text{softmax}(z_2) = \frac{2.718}{11.212} \approx 0.242$$

$$\text{softmax}(z_3) = \frac{1.105}{11.212} \approx 0.099$$

**Step 4:** Verify the sum.

$$0.659 + 0.242 + 0.099 = 1.000 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation — class 1 has $\approx 65.9\%$ probability, class 2 has $\approx 24.2\%$, class 3 has $\approx 9.9\%$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Stability Tip:** For very large logits like $\mathbf{z} = (1000, 999, 998)$, computing $e^{1000}$ overflows floating-point. Always subtract the maximum logit first:
> $$\text{softmax}(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i - \max_j z_j}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j - \max_j z_j}}$$

---

## 3.4 Gradient Behaviour: Why ReLU Wins in Deep Networks

| $z$ | $\sigma'(z)$ | $\tanh'(z)$ | ReLU$'(z)$ | LReLU$'(z)$ ($\alpha = 0.01$) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $-10$ | $\approx 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$ | $\approx 2.0 \times 10^{-9}$ | $0$ | $0.01$ |
| $-2$ | $0.105$ | $0.071$ | $0$ | $0.01$ |
| $0$ | $0.250$ | $1.000$ | undefined | undefined |
| $+2$ | $0.105$ | $0.071$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| $+10$ | $\approx 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$ | $\approx 2.0 \times 10^{-9}$ | $1$ | $1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Notice that for $|z| > 2$, sigmoid and tanh gradients fall below $0.1$. In a network with 10+ layers, multiplying 10 such small gradients yields $\sim 10^{-10}$ — the **vanishing gradient problem** that ReLU solves by maintaining gradient $= 1$ for all positive pre-activations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📙 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Neuron Architecture with Activation Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1[x1] --> sumNode
    in2[x2] --> sumNode
    in3[x3] --> sumNode
    w1[w1] --> sumNode
    w2[w2] --> sumNode
    w3[w3] --> sumNode
    bias[b] --> sumNode
    sumNode[Sum and Bias Node] --> actNode{Activation Function}
    actNode --> outA[a equals f of z]
    outA --> outNode[Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram shows the standard neuron model: weighted sum of inputs + bias produces the pre-activation $z$, which is then transformed by the activation function $f$ to produce output $a$.

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Activation Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Choose Activation Function] --> q1{Output layer of network}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Binary or Multi class}
    q1 -- No --> q3{Hidden layer of deep network}
    q2 -- Binary --> sigA[Sigmoid]
    q2 -- Multi class --> softA[Softmax]
    q3 -- Default --> reluA[ReLU]
    q3 -- Dying neurons observed --> leakyA[Leaky ReLU or PReLU]
    q3 -- RNN or LSTM --> tanhA[Tanh]
    q3 -- Classic perceptron --> signA[Sign]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SF[Sign Function]
        s1[Input z] --> s2{if z greater than 0}
        s2 -- Yes --> s3[Output plus 1]
        s2 -- No --> s4[Output minus 1]
    end
    subgraph SGF[Sigmoid]
        g1[Input z] --> g2[Compute 1 over 1 plus e to power minus z]
        g2 --> g3[Output in open interval 0 1]
    end
    subgraph TNH[Tanh]
        t1[Input z] --> t2[Compute e power z minus e power minus z over e power z plus e power minus z]
        t2 --> t3[Output in open interval minus 1 plus 1]
    end
    subgraph RLU[ReLU]
        r1[Input z] --> r2{if z greater than 0}
        r2 -- Yes --> r3[Output z]
        r2 -- No --> r4[Output 0]
    end
    subgraph LRL[Leaky ReLU]
        l1[Input z] --> l2{if z greater than 0}
        l2 -- Yes --> l3[Output z]
        l2 -- No --> l4[Output alpha times z]
    end
    subgraph SFT[Softmax]
        f1[Input vector z] --> f2[Exponentiate each element]
        f2 --> f3[Normalize by sum of all exponentials]
        f3 --> f4[Probability vector]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 The Vanishing Gradient Problem — Visual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA[Input] --> L1[Layer 1]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5]
    L5 --> outA[Output]
    outA -.Backprop gradient.-> L5
    L5 -.Multiply by sigmoid prime.-> L4
    L4 -.Multiply by sigmoid prime.-> L3
    L3 -.Multiply by sigmoid prime.-> L2
    L2 -.Multiply by sigmoid prime.-> L1
    L1 -.Almost zero gradient.-> inpA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** With sigmoid activations, the backpropagated gradient gets multiplied by $\sigma'(z) \le 0.25$ at every layer. After 5 layers, the gradient is at most $0.25^5 \approx 9.8 \times 10^{-4}$. Early layers learn extremely slowly — this is the **vanishing gradient problem**. ReLU's gradient of $1$ in the positive region eliminates this chain of decay.

---

## 4.5 Output Range Visualization (Mermaid Chart-style Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sig[Input z] --> sigOut[Output in 0 to 1]
    tan[Input z] --> tanOut[Output in minus 1 to plus 1]
    rel[Input z] --> relOut[Output in 0 to infinity]
    lrl[Input z] --> lrlOut[Output in minus infinity to plus infinity]
    sgn[Input z] --> sgnOut[Output in minus 1 or plus 1]
    sft[Input vector] --> sftOut[Output in 0 to 1 summing to 1]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📒 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define the sigmoid activation function. State its two main drawbacks when used in hidden layers of deep networks.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Definition (1 mark):** The sigmoid (logistic) function is defined as:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

It maps any real-valued input $z$ to the open interval $(0, 1)$.

**Drawback 1 — Vanishing Gradient (1 mark):** For large positive or large negative $z$, the gradient $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ approaches $0$. In a deep network, multiplying many small gradients across layers causes early layers to receive almost no learning signal.

**Drawback 2 — Non-zero-centered output (1 mark):** The output range is $(0, 1)$, never negative. This means gradients w.r.t. weights are always either all-positive or all-negative (depending on the sign of the upstream gradient), leading to inefficient zig-zag updates during gradient descent.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Briefly explain the "Dying ReLU" problem and how Leaky ReLU addresses it.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Dying ReLU — Explanation (1.5 marks):** During training, if a neuron's weights are updated such that its pre-activation $z$ is always negative for every input in the training set, then $\text{ReLU}(z) = 0$ always, and the gradient $\text{ReLU}'(z) = 0$ as well. Since no gradient flows back through this neuron, its weights never get updated again, and the neuron is permanently "dead" — contributing nothing to the network.

**How Leaky ReLU fixes it (1.5 marks):** Leaky ReLU is defined as $f(z) = \alpha z$ for $z < 0$, where $\alpha$ is a small positive constant (typically $0.01$). This ensures that even when the neuron is in the negative region, it still produces a small non-zero output and — more importantly — a non-zero gradient $\alpha$. The neuron can therefore still be updated by backpropagation and recover from "dying."

---

## 📝 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the derivative of the sigmoid activation function $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ and show that it can be expressed purely in terms of $\sigma(z)$ itself. Hence, compute the gradient of a sigmoid neuron with respect to its input for $z = 2.0$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the Softmax activation function with its mathematical formulation. For a 3-class classifier, given the output logits $\mathbf{z} = (2.0, 1.0, 0.1)$, compute the softmax probabilities. Comment on the numerical stability issue and how it is resolved.

---

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Sigmoid Derivative [7 marks]

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** Start from the definition.

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 2 [1 mark]:** Rewrite as $\sigma(z) = (1 + e^{-z})^{-1}$ and apply the chain rule.

$$\sigma'(z) = -(1 + e^{-z})^{-2} \cdot \frac{d}{dz}(1 + e^{-z})$$

**Step 3 [1 mark]:** Differentiate the inner term.

$$\frac{d}{dz}(1 + e^{-z}) = -e^{-z}$$

**Step 4 [1 mark]:** Substitute.

$$\sigma'(z) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^{2}}$$

**Step 5 [1 mark]:** Split as product of $\sigma(z)$ and another term.

$$\sigma'(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 6 [1 mark]:** Simplify the second term to get the closed-form identity.

$$\frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = 1 - \sigma(z)$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)\;}$$

**Step 7 [1 mark]:** Numerical evaluation at $z = 2.0$.

- $\sigma(2.0) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-2}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1353} = \frac{1}{1.1353} \approx 0.8808$
- $\sigma'(2.0) = 0.8808 \times (1 - 0.8808) = 0.8808 \times 0.1192 \approx 0.1050$

So the gradient is approximately $\mathbf{0.105}$.

---

### Part (b) — Softmax Function [7 marks]

**Step 1 [2 marks]:** Definition.

> The softmax function converts a $K$-dimensional vector of real-valued logits $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, \ldots, z_K)$ into a probability distribution over $K$ mutually exclusive classes:
> $$\text{softmax}(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, K$$
> Outputs are all strictly positive and sum to $1$.

**Step 2 [2 marks]:** Compute exponentials for $\mathbf{z} = (2.0, 1.0, 0.1)$.

$$e^{2.0} \approx 7.389, \quad e^{1.0} \approx 2.718, \quad e^{0.1} \approx 1.105$$

**Step 3 [1 mark]:** Sum them.

$$\sum e^{z_j} = 7.389 + 2.718 + 1.105 = 11.212$$

**Step 4 [1 mark]:** Compute the softmax outputs.

$$P(y=1) = \frac{7.389}{11.212} \approx 0.659$$

$$P(y=2) = \frac{2.718}{11.212} \approx 0.242$$

$$P(y=3) = \frac{1.105}{11.212} \approx 0.099$$

**Step 5 [1 mark]:** Numerical stability comment.

> For large logits (e.g., $z_i \sim 1000$), $e^{z_i}$ overflows floating-point. The fix is to subtract $\max_j z_j$ from all logits before exponentiating:
> $$\text{softmax}(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i - M}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j - M}}, \quad M = \max_j z_j$$
> This is mathematically equivalent because the shift cancels in the ratio.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the structure of a biological-to-artificial neuron mapping. List any four activation functions used in artificial neural networks and state one key property of each.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare ReLU and Sigmoid activation functions across the dimensions of (i) computational cost, (ii) gradient behaviour, (iii) effect of vanishing gradient, (iv) output range, and (v) zero-centeredness. State which is the default choice for hidden layers in modern deep networks and justify your answer.

---

#### Model Solution Outline:

### Part (a) — Neuron Mapping & Activation Functions [7 marks]

**Diagram description (3 marks):** A clear hand-drawn or text-art block diagram showing:
- **Biological neuron** components: dendrites → cell body → axon → synaptic terminals.
- **Artificial neuron** mapping: inputs $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ → weights $w_1, \ldots, w_n$ → summation $\sum w_i x_i + b$ → activation function $f$ → output $a$.

**Four activation functions with one key property each (4 marks, 1 mark each):**

| Activation | One Key Property |
|---|---|
| **Sigmoid** | Output range $(0,1)$ — interpretable as probability |
| **Tanh** | Zero-centered output range $(-1, 1)$ |
| **ReLU** | Non-saturating for $z > 0$ (gradient = 1), computationally cheap |
| **Softmax** | Outputs form a valid probability distribution summing to 1 |

### Part (b) — ReLU vs Sigmoid [7 marks]

| Dimension (1.4 marks each) | Sigmoid | ReLU |
|---|---|---|
| **Computational cost** | Expensive — requires computing $e^{-z}$ | Cheap — single $\max(0, z)$ operation |
| **Gradient behaviour** | Maximum gradient $= 0.25$ (at $z=0$), saturates elsewhere | Gradient $= 1$ for $z>0$, $= 0$ for $z<0$ |
| **Vanishing gradient** | Severe — gradients $\to 0$ for large $\vert z \vert$ | Absent in the positive region |
| **Output range** | $(0, 1)$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| **Zero-centeredness** | Not zero-centered (all positive outputs) | Not zero-centered (all non-negative outputs) |

**Default choice (built into the 7-mark answer):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ReLU is the default activation for hidden layers in modern deep networks.** Justification: (i) cheap forward/backward computation, (ii) gradient is exactly $1$ in the positive regime, eliminating the vanishing gradient problem in deep stacks, (iii) induces sparsity, which often improves generalization.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Activation Function Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the "range"** of an activation function in a definition question — examiners expect both the formula AND the range. Always state it explicitly. ($-1$ mark)
> 2. **Confusing the sign function with the Heaviside step function** — Sign returns $\{-1, +1\}$; Heaviside returns $\{0, 1\}$. They are *not* the same! ($-1$ mark)
> 3. **Stating ReLU is differentiable everywhere** — it is **NOT** differentiable at $z=0$. Use the term "sub-differentiable" or "differentiable almost everywhere" with sub-gradient form. ($-1$ mark)
> 4. **Forgetting numerical stability in softmax** — in any numerical computation question, always mention the $\max$-subtraction trick. ($-1$ mark)
> 5. **Claiming sigmoid is zero-centered** — it is NOT (output range is $(0, 1)$). Tanh is. This is a very common KTU trap. ($-1$ mark)
> 6. **Writing Leaky ReLU gradient as 0 on the negative side** — it is $\alpha$ (typically $0.01$), not $0$. This is the *whole point* of Leaky ReLU! ($-1$ mark)
> 7. **Not drawing the activation curves** when asked for a "neat diagram" in 7-mark questions — examiners explicitly require a graphical illustration. ($-2$ marks)

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1**
>
> ✅ **Sign function** $f(z) = \text{sign}(z)$: Output $\{-1, +1\}$; non-differentiable; used in classic perceptron only.
>
> ✅ **Sigmoid** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$: Output $(0, 1)$; derivative is $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$; **not zero-centered**; suffers from **vanishing gradient**; used in **binary classification output layer**.
>
> ✅ **Tanh** $\tanh(z) = \frac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}}$: Output $(-1, 1)$; **zero-centered**; derivative is $1 - \tanh^2(z)$; maximum gradient is $1$ (at $z=0$); preferred over sigmoid for hidden layers of shallow/RNN networks.
>
> ✅ **ReLU** $f(z) = \max(0, z)$: Output $[0, \infty)$; **non-saturating** for $z>0$; derivative is $1$ for $z>0$ and $0$ for $z<0$; **computationally cheap**; **induces sparsity**; suffers from **Dying ReLU** problem; **default for deep network hidden layers**.
>
> ✅ **Leaky ReLU** $f(z) = \max(\alpha z, z)$ with $\alpha \in (0, 1)$: Output $(-\infty, \infty)$; derivative is $1$ for $z>0$ and $\alpha$ for $z<0$; **fixes Dying ReLU** by allowing a small gradient on the negative side; variants include **PReLU** (learnable $\alpha$) and **RReLU** (randomized $\alpha$).
>
> ✅ **Softmax** $\text{softmax}(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$: Output is a **probability distribution** (each in $(0,1)$, sum to $1$); **generalizes sigmoid to multi-class**; requires **numerical stability trick** (subtract $\max$); used in **multi-class classification output layer**; derivative is the **Jacobian matrix** $S_i(\delta_{ij} - S_j)$.
>
> ✅ **Universal Approximation Theorem**: Non-linear activations give neural networks the power to approximate **any continuous function**.
>
> ✅ **Vanishing Gradient Problem**: Multiply $\sigma'(z) \le 0.25$ across many layers $\Rightarrow$ early layers receive near-zero gradient; ReLU solves this with constant gradient $1$ for $z > 0$.
>
> ✅ **Dying ReLU Problem**: When a neuron's weights cause $z < 0$ for all inputs, the gradient is $0$ forever; Leaky ReLU prevents this.
>
> ✅ **Selection Rules** (high-yield for exam):
> - Binary output $\rightarrow$ **Sigmoid**
> - Multi-class output $\rightarrow$ **Softmax**
> - Hidden layer (deep) $\rightarrow$ **ReLU**
> - Hidden layer (RNN) $\rightarrow$ **Tanh**
> - Hidden layer (perceptron-style) $\rightarrow$ **Sign**
> - Dying neurons observed $\rightarrow$ **Leaky ReLU / PReLU**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture of Neural Networks: Feedforward and Feedback Neural Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT403 - MACHINE LEARNING II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Architecture of Neural Networks: Feedforward and Feedback Neural Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Neural Network — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Neural Network (NN)** is a computational model inspired by the structure and functioning of biological neurons in the human brain. It consists of interconnected processing elements called **artificial neurons (nodes)** organized in **layers**, which learn complex, non-linear mappings from inputs to outputs by adjusting connection weights through training.

Mathematically, a neural network implements a parameterized function:

$$f_{\theta} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{m}, \quad \theta = \{W, b\}$$

where $W$ represents the set of all weight matrices and $b$ represents the bias vectors across layers.

### 1.2 The Two Principal Architectures

Neural networks are broadly classified by the **direction and nature of information flow** between layers:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Classification:**
> 1. **Feedforward Neural Networks (FNN)** — Strictly **acyclic** (no loops). Data flows from input to output in **one direction only**. Also called **Acyclic Neural Networks**.
> 2. **Feedback Neural Networks (FBNN)** — Contain **cycles / recurrent connections**. Information can flow back from later layers to earlier ones. The most prominent class is the **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

| Network Type | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Feedforward NN** | A **factory assembly line** — raw materials (inputs) enter at one end, are progressively transformed at each workstation (hidden layer), and finished goods (outputs) emerge at the other end. Nothing goes backwards. |
| **Feedback NN** | A **GPS navigation system** — your current location (current input) is interpreted not just by the map, but also by your **memory of where you have been** (previous hidden state). The system constantly updates its understanding based on history. |

### 1.4 Why Two Architectures? — Motivation

- **FNN**: Best for **static, independent data** — images, tabular data, simple classification/regression where each input is treated in isolation.
- **FBNN**: Best for **sequential or temporal data** — speech, text, time-series, video — where the **order and history of inputs** carry semantic meaning.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** *"Feedforward = Feed-Forward Only. Feedback = Feedback Allowed (loops)."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Feedforward Neural Network (FNN) — Architecture & Theory

#### 2.1.1 Structural Components

A standard FNN (also called **Multi-Layer Perceptron, MLP**) consists of:

1. **Input Layer** — Distributes input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. No computation occurs here.
2. **One or more Hidden Layers** — Each layer $l$ applies an affine transformation followed by a non-linear **activation function**.
3. **Output Layer** — Produces final prediction $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$.

#### 2.1.2 Operational Logic — Layer-by-Layer Forward Pass

For a network with $L$ layers, the forward propagation proceeds as:

- **Step 1:** Receive input $\mathbf{x}$.
- **Step 2:** At each layer $l \in \{1, 2, \ldots, L\}$, compute the **pre-activation** (linear combination):

$$\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = W^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$$

- **Step 3:** Apply the **activation function** $f$ to get the layer output:

$$\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = f^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$$

- **Step 4:** Propagate $\mathbf{a}^{(l)}$ as input to the next layer. For $l=1$, $\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$.
- **Step 5:** Final layer output is the network prediction $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}^{(L)}$.

#### 2.1.3 Common Activation Functions

| Function | Formula | Range | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | Binary classification output |
| **Tanh** | $\tanh(z) = \dfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | $(-1, 1)$ | Hidden layers (zero-centered) |
| **ReLU** | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Default hidden layer choice |
| **Softmax** | $\text{softmax}(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | $(0, 1)$, sums to 1 | Multi-class output |

#### 2.1.4 The "Why" — Universal Approximation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Approximation Theorem (Cybenko, 1989):**
> A feedforward network with **a single hidden layer containing a finite number of neurons** and **any non-linear, bounded activation function** can approximate **any continuous function** on a compact domain to arbitrary precision. This is the **theoretical foundation** of FNN power.

### 2.2 Feedback (Recurrent) Neural Network — Architecture & Theory

#### 2.2.1 Structural Components

A Feedback NN introduces **recurrent edges** — connections from a neuron back to itself or to neurons in previous layers. This creates an **internal memory state** that evolves over time.

#### 2.2.2 Operational Logic — Unrolled Through Time

At each time step $t$, the hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ is updated using the current input $\mathbf{x}_t$ and the **previous hidden state** $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$:

- **Step 1:** Receive input $\mathbf{x}_t$ and previous state $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$.
- **Step 2:** Compute new hidden state:

$$\mathbf{h}_t = f_h\!\left(W_{xh}\,\mathbf{x}_t + W_{hh}\,\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h\right)$$

- **Step 3:** Compute output:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = f_o\!\left(W_{hy}\,\mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y\right)$$

where $W_{xh}$, $W_{hh}$, $W_{hy}$ are the **input-to-hidden**, **hidden-to-hidden (recurrent)**, and **hidden-to-output** weight matrices respectively.

#### 2.2.3 Weight Sharing — The Key FBNN Property

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike FNN where weights differ at every layer, in FBNN the **same weight matrices are reused at every time step**. This makes FBNN parameter-efficient and capable of handling variable-length sequences.

#### 2.2.4 Training: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

FBNNs are trained by **unrolling the network across all time steps** and applying standard backpropagation. Gradients flow backwards through both **layer depth** and **time**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table — these equations appear in 80% of KTU module questions.**

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **FNN Pre-activation** | $\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = W^{(l)}\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$ | Linear transform at layer $l$ |
| **FNN Activation** | $\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = f(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$ | Non-linear mapping |
| **FBNN Hidden Update** | $\mathbf{h}_t = f_h(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + W_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h)$ | State evolution |
| **FBNN Output** | $\mathbf{y}_t = f_o(W_{hy}\mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y)$ | Time-step prediction |
| **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Squash to $(0,1)$ |
| **Sigmoid Derivative** | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\left[1 - \sigma(z)\right]$ | Backprop |
| **Tanh** | $\tanh(z) = \dfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | Squash to $(-1,1)$ |
| **ReLU** | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Sparse activation |
| **MSE Loss** | $L = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_i - \hat{y}_i\right)^{2}$ | Regression error |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $L = -\sum_{i} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Classification error |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FNN**: Medical diagnosis from patient features, image classification, credit scoring, sensor-based quality control in manufacturing.
- **FBNN**: Stock price prediction, speech recognition (Siri, Alexa), machine translation (Google Translate), anomaly detection in IoT time-series, autonomous driving trajectory prediction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Pass of a 2-Layer FNN

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup (KTU Standard):** Given input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$, hidden layer with 4 neurons (ReLU activation), and output layer with 2 neurons (Sigmoid activation), derive the network output symbolically.

**Step 1 — Input Layer (No Computation):**
The input is simply passed forward.

$$\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Hidden Layer Pre-activation:**
The hidden layer weight matrix $W^{(1)}$ has dimensions $4 \times 3$ and bias $\mathbf{b}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{4}$.

$$\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = W^{(1)} \mathbf{a}^{(0)} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$$

Expanding explicitly:

$$z^{(1)}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{3} W^{(1)}_{ji}\,x_i + b^{(1)}_j, \quad j = 1, 2, 3, 4$$

**Step 3 — Hidden Layer Activation (ReLU):**

$$a^{(1)}_j = \text{ReLU}(z^{(1)}_j) = \max\!\left(0, \sum_{i=1}^{3} W^{(1)}_{ji}\,x_i + b^{(1)}_j\right)$$

**Step 4 — Output Layer Pre-activation:**
The output layer weight matrix $W^{(2)}$ has dimensions $2 \times 4$ and bias $\mathbf{b}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$.

$$\mathbf{z}^{(2)} = W^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$$

**Step 5 — Output Layer Activation (Sigmoid):**

$$\hat{y}_k = \sigma(z^{(2)}_k) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp\!\left(-\sum_{j=1}^{4} W^{(2)}_{kj}\,a^{(1)}_j - b^{(2)}_k\right)}, \quad k = 1, 2$$

**Step 6 — Final Network Output:**

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{y}_1 \\ \hat{y}_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the complete end-to-end forward pass — every intermediate quantity is computed in sequence.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: FBNN Hidden State Update — Unrolled Across 3 Time Steps

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup:** A simple RNN processes an input sequence $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \mathbf{x}_3$ with initial hidden state $\mathbf{h}_0 = \mathbf{0}$. Derive the hidden states at $t = 1, 2, 3$.

**Step 1 — Time Step $t = 1$:**

$$\mathbf{h}_1 = f_h\!\left(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_1 + W_{hh}\mathbf{h}_0 + \mathbf{b}_h\right)$$

Since $\mathbf{h}_0 = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\mathbf{h}_1 = f_h\!\left(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{b}_h\right)$$

**Step 2 — Time Step $t = 2$:**

$$\mathbf{h}_2 = f_h\!\left(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_2 + W_{hh}\mathbf{h}_1 + \mathbf{b}_h\right)$$

Substituting the expression for $\mathbf{h}_1$:

$$\mathbf{h}_2 = f_h\!\left(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_2 + W_{hh}\,f_h\!\left(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{b}_h\right) + \mathbf{b}_h\right)$$

**Step 3 — Time Step $t = 3$:**

$$\mathbf{h}_3 = f_h\!\left(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_3 + W_{hh}\mathbf{h}_2 + \mathbf{b}_h\right)$$

Full substitution:

$$\mathbf{h}_3 = f_h\!\left(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_3 + W_{hh}\,f_h\!\left(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_2 + W_{hh}\,f_h\!\left(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{b}_h\right) + \mathbf{b}_h\right) + \mathbf{b}_h\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** Each $\mathbf{h}_t$ is a function of **all previous inputs** $\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_t$. This is precisely how the network "remembers" history — a property absent in FNN.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — FNN vs FBNN Comparison

> [!TIP]
> **This comparative table is a high-weightage KTU question type — expect 7 to 14 mark questions.**

| Property | Feedforward NN (FNN) | Feedback NN (FBNN / RNN) |
|---|---|---|
| **Information Flow** | One direction: input $\rightarrow$ output | Loops / cycles permitted; output depends on past |
| **Graph Structure** | Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) | Directed Cyclic Graph (DCG) |
| **Memory** | No internal state / memory | Maintains hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ as memory |
| **Input-Output Mapping** | One-to-one (fixed size) | One-to-many, many-to-one, many-to-many |
| **Best For** | Static / independent data (images, tables) | Sequential / temporal data (text, speech, video) |
| **Weight Sharing** | No (different weights per layer) | Yes (same $W_{xh}, W_{hh}$ at every time step) |
| **Training Method** | Backpropagation | Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) |
| **Vanishing Gradient** | Less severe in shallow nets | Severe due to repeated multiplication of $W_{hh}$ |
| **Examples** | MLP, CNN | Vanilla RNN, LSTM, GRU |
| **Computational Cost** | Low to moderate | High (sequential, hard to parallelize) |
| **Output at** | Single forward pass | One output per time step |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Vanishing Gradient in FBNN (Theoretical Justification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a frequently asked 7-mark question in KTU exams.**

Consider the gradient of loss $L_t$ at time $t$ with respect to weight $W_{hh}$. By the chain rule across $k$ time steps:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

The term $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}$ involves $t-k$ recurrent multiplications:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W_{hh}^{\top}\,\text{diag}\!\left(f'_h(\mathbf{z}_i)\right)$$

Taking the norm:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \right\Vert \leq \left(\Vert W_{hh}\Vert \cdot \max \vert f'_h \vert\right)^{t-k}$$

- If $\Vert W_{hh}\Vert \cdot \max \vert f'_h \vert < 1$, the gradient **vanishes exponentially** with $t-k$.
- If $\Vert W_{hh}\Vert \cdot \max \vert f'_h \vert > 1$, the gradient **explodes exponentially** with $t-k$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FNN is less affected:** In FNN, depth is typically shallow, and gradient flows through only a few layers, not through repeated multiplications of the same matrix.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Feedforward Neural Network (FNN)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[INPUT LAYER n1 n2 n3] --> B[HIDDEN LAYER H1 4 NEURONS]
    B --> C[HIDDEN LAYER H2 4 NEURONS]
    C --> D[OUTPUT LAYER O1 O2 SIGMOID]
    B --> E[ReLU ACTIVATION]
    C --> F[ReLU ACTIVATION]
    D --> G[CLASS PROBABILITIES]
    A --> A1[X1 X2 X3 INPUT VECTOR]
    D --> D1[Y HAT 1 Y HAT 2 OUTPUT]

    style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- Blue block = **Input Layer** (3 features).
- Green blocks = **Hidden Layers** (4 neurons each, with ReLU).
- Orange block = **Output Layer** (2 neurons, Sigmoid for binary output).
- Purple blocks = **Non-linear activation functions** applied at each layer.
- Arrows flow **strictly left-to-right** — no cycles. This is the defining property of FNN.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Feedback Neural Network (FBNN) Unrolled Through Time

```mermaid
graph LR
    X1[X1 T EQUALS 1] --> H1[H1 T EQUALS 1]
    X2[X2 T EQUALS 2] --> H2[H2 T EQUALS 2]
    X3[X3 T EQUALS 3] --> H3[H3 T EQUALS 3]
    H1 --> Y1[Y1 T EQUALS 1]
    H2 --> Y2[Y2 T EQUALS 2]
    H3 --> Y3[Y3 T EQUALS 3]
    H1 -->|W_hh RECURRENT| H2
    H2 -->|W_hh RECURRENT| H3
    H0[H0 EQUALS 0 INITIAL STATE] --> H1

    style X1 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style X2 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style X3 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H1 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H2 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H3 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H0 fill:#9013FE,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Y1 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Y2 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Y3 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- Blue blocks = **Input at each time step** $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \mathbf{x}_3$.
- Green blocks = **Hidden states** $\mathbf{h}_1, \mathbf{h}_2, \mathbf{h}_3$ — these form the "memory".
- Orange blocks = **Outputs** $\mathbf{y}_1, \mathbf{y}_2, \mathbf{y}_3$ — one prediction per time step.
- Purple block = **Initial hidden state** $\mathbf{h}_0 = \mathbf{0}$.
- The arrows labelled **"W_hh RECURRENT"** are the **feedback connections** — this is the defining feature of FBNN. The same weight matrix $W_{hh}$ is shared across all time steps.

---

### 4.3 Comparative Block Architecture — FNN vs FBNN (Single Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SUB1[FNN ARCHITECTURE ACYCLIC]
        direction LR
        A1[INPUT X] --> B1[HIDDEN LAYER] --> C1[OUTPUT Y HAT]
    end

    subgraph SUB2[FBNN ARCHITECTURE CYCLIC]
        direction LR
        A2[INPUT XT] --> B2[HIDDEN HT]
        B2 --> C2[OUTPUT YT]
        B2 -->|RECURRENT LOOP| B2
    end

    style A1 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B1 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B2 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Side-by-side contrast:**
- **FNN (left):** Clean linear pipeline — input $\rightarrow$ hidden $\rightarrow$ output.
- **FBNN (right):** Hidden state has a **self-loop** (recurrent connection), meaning the current state depends on its previous value.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Feedforward and Feedback Neural Networks with suitable examples. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Feedforward NN | Feedback NN (RNN) |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal Flow** | Unidirectional, input to output | Bidirectional / cyclic connections exist |
| **Memory** | No memory of past inputs | Maintains hidden state as memory |
| **Topology** | Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) | Directed Cyclic Graph |
| **Example** | Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) for digit classification | Vanilla RNN for next-word prediction |

*(Any 3 valid distinguishing points — full 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define the term "activation function" in a neural network. List any two commonly used activation functions. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
An **activation function** is a mathematical function applied to the pre-activation of a neuron to introduce **non-linearity** into the network, enabling it to learn complex, non-linear mappings between input and output.

$$\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = f(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$$

Two common activation functions:
1. **Sigmoid:** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$, range $(0, 1)$.
2. **ReLU:** $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$, range $[0, \infty)$.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Feedforward Neural Network (Multi-Layer Perceptron)**. Clearly label the input, hidden, and output layers. Describe the role of weights, biases, and activation functions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **complete forward propagation equations** for a 2-hidden-layer FNN with input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, first hidden layer of size $h_1$, second hidden layer of size $h_2$, and output $\hat{\mathbf{y}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$. Use ReLU in hidden layers and Sigmoid in the output layer. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Architecture of MLP:**

A Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) is the canonical Feedforward Neural Network. It consists of:

1. **Input Layer** — Receives the feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. No computation, just distribution.
2. **Hidden Layers** — One or more layers where each neuron computes a weighted sum of all inputs from the previous layer, adds a bias, and applies a non-linear activation function.
3. **Output Layer** — Produces the final prediction $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$.

**Role of Components:**
- **Weights ($W$):** Learnable parameters that scale the input signals — determine the "strength" of connections.
- **Biases ($b$):** Learnable offsets that shift the activation function — allow the model to fit data not passing through the origin.
- **Activation Function ($f$):** Introduces non-linearity; without it, the entire network collapses to a single linear transformation regardless of depth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's note:** A clear labelled diagram (similar to Section 4.1 of these notes) is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Input Layer:**
$$\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$$

**Step 2 — First Hidden Layer Pre-activation:**
Weight matrix $W^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h_1 \times n}$, bias $\mathbf{b}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h_1}$:

$$\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = W^{(1)}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$$

**Step 3 — First Hidden Layer Activation (ReLU):**

$$\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \text{ReLU}(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}) = \max(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{z}^{(1)})$$

**Step 4 — Second Hidden Layer Pre-activation:**
Weight matrix $W^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h_2 \times h_1}$, bias $\mathbf{b}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h_2}$:

$$\mathbf{z}^{(2)} = W^{(2)}\mathbf{a}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$$

**Step 5 — Second Hidden Layer Activation (ReLU):**

$$\mathbf{a}^{(2)} = \text{ReLU}(\mathbf{z}^{(2)}) = \max(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{z}^{(2)})$$

**Step 6 — Output Layer Pre-activation:**
Weight matrix $W^{(3)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times h_2}$, bias $\mathbf{b}^{(3)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$:

$$\mathbf{z}^{(3)} = W^{(3)}\mathbf{a}^{(2)} + \mathbf{b}^{(3)}$$

**Step 7 — Output Layer Activation (Sigmoid):**

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(3)}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\mathbf{z}^{(3)}}}$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{y}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the final output vector.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's tip:** Notice the **dimensional cascade** — each $W^{(l)}$ is shaped to map the previous layer's output dimension to the next. Marking schemes often award marks for explicitly stating dimensions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of a Feedback (Recurrent) Neural Network**. How does it differ from a Feedforward network in handling sequential data? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **hidden state update equations** for a vanilla RNN unrolled across 3 time steps, and explain why FBNNs suffer from the **vanishing gradient problem**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Architecture of FBNN (RNN):**

A Recurrent Neural Network is designed to process **sequential data** by maintaining a **hidden state** $\mathbf{h}_t$ that captures information about all previously seen inputs.

**Core Components:**
- **Input sequence:** $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_T$
- **Hidden state sequence:** $\mathbf{h}_1, \mathbf{h}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{h}_T$ (the "memory")
- **Output sequence:** $\mathbf{y}_1, \mathbf{y}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{y}_T$
- **Three weight matrices:** $W_{xh}$ (input-to-hidden), $W_{hh}$ (hidden-to-hidden, recurrent), $W_{hy}$ (hidden-to-output).

**Difference from FNN in handling sequential data:**
- FNN treats each input **independently** — there is no notion of "previous" input. To process a sequence, the entire sequence would need to be fed in as one large vector, which is **inefficient** and **order-agnostic**.
- FBNN processes the sequence **one element at a time**, and the **hidden state** carries a summary of all past elements. This makes FBNN **order-sensitive** and **parameter-efficient** (same weights are reused at each step).

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Time Step $t = 1$ (with $\mathbf{h}_0 = \mathbf{0}$):**

$$\mathbf{h}_1 = f_h(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_1 + W_{hh}\mathbf{h}_0 + \mathbf{b}_h) = f_h(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{b}_h)$$

**Step 2 — Time Step $t = 2$:**

$$\mathbf{h}_2 = f_h(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_2 + W_{hh}\mathbf{h}_1 + \mathbf{b}_h)$$

**Step 3 — Time Step $t = 3$:**

$$\mathbf{h}_3 = f_h(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_3 + W_{hh}\mathbf{h}_2 + \mathbf{b}_h)$$

**Step 4 — Vanishing Gradient Explanation:**

The gradient of the loss at time $t=3$ with respect to $W_{hh}$ requires backpropagation through time, involving the chain rule across all previous time steps:

$$\frac{\partial L_3}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{3} \frac{\partial L_3}{\partial \mathbf{h}_3} \cdot \prod_{i=k+1}^{3} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

The product term $\prod \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}}$ involves repeated multiplication of $W_{hh}^{\top}$ and the derivative of the activation function. As shown in Section 3.4, the norm decays (or explodes) exponentially:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \right\Vert \leq \left(\Vert W_{hh}\Vert \cdot \max \vert f'_h \vert\right)^{t-k}$$

If this factor is less than 1, **gradients vanish** — earlier time steps receive negligibly small updates and the network fails to learn long-range dependencies. This is the **vanishing gradient problem**, the central weakness of vanilla RNNs and the motivation for **LSTM** and **GRU** architectures.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing MLP with deep learning:** Students often write "neural network" and "deep learning" interchangeably. MLP with 1 hidden layer is NOT deep; FBNN with unrolled time steps IS deep.
> 2. **Forgetting dimensions in derivations:** KTU examiners explicitly check for correct matrix dimensions in $W_{xh}$ and $W_{hh}$. Always state $W_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$ and $W_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$.
> 3. **Missing the role of activation function:** Several students derive only the linear part $\mathbf{z} = W\mathbf{x} + b$ and forget to apply $f(\cdot)$ — this is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU schemes.
> 4. **Vanishing vs Exploding gradient confusion:** Both stem from the same mathematical origin (repeated multiplication of Jacobians). State the **threshold condition** explicitly to earn full marks.
> 5. **Drawing FNN without labels:** A diagram with arrows but no layer names, no neuron counts, and no activation function labels is awarded **zero marks** for the diagram portion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final-minute revision — read this section 3 times before the exam.**

- ✅ **FNN = Feed-Forward Only** — No cycles, no memory, processes each input independently.
- ✅ **FBNN = Feedback Allowed** — Has cycles (recurrent connections) and maintains a hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ as memory.
- ✅ **Universal Approximation Theorem** — A 1-hidden-layer FNN with non-linear activation can approximate any continuous function.
- ✅ **Two equations define FBNN:** $\mathbf{h}_t = f_h(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + W_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h)$ and $\mathbf{y}_t = f_o(W_{hy}\mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y)$.
- ✅ **Weight sharing** is the key FBNN property — $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ are reused at every time step.
- ✅ **FNN training** uses standard backpropagation; **FBNN training** uses Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT).
- ✅ **Vanishing gradient** in FBNN arises from repeated multiplication of $W_{hh}$ Jacobians — gradient decays exponentially with time-step distance.
- ✅ **Sigmoid output** $\rightarrow$ binary classification; **Softmax output** $\rightarrow$ multi-class; **linear output** $\rightarrow$ regression.
- ✅ **FNN is preferred for** static data (images, tabular); **FBNN is preferred for** sequential data (text, speech, time-series).
- ✅ **Activation functions are non-negotiable** — without them, stacking layers is mathematically equivalent to a single linear layer.
- ✅ **Dimensions matter in KTU** — always annotate matrix dimensions like $W^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l \times n_{l-1}}$ to earn full derivation marks.
- ✅ **LSTM and GRU** are advanced FBNN variants designed specifically to overcome the vanishing gradient problem — expect them in follow-up questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Layer Perceptron
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT403 - MACHINE LEARNING II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single Layer Perceptron

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Single Layer Perceptron (SLP)** is the most fundamental and architecturally simplest form of a feedforward artificial neural network. It consists of a single layer of artificial neurons (also called *Linear Threshold Units* or *McCulloch-Pitts neurons*) that directly project from the input layer to the output layer, with **no hidden layers** present between them. The model was proposed by **Frank Rosenblatt in 1957** at the Cornell Aeronautical Laboratory.

Mathematically, a Single Layer Perceptron with $n$ inputs implements the decision function:

$$y = \phi\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)$$

where $x_i$ are the input features, $w_i$ are the learnable synaptic weights, $b$ is the bias term, and $\phi$ is a non-linear activation (typically a step function).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Single Layer" refers strictly to the **computational layer of neurons**, not the input layer. The input nodes in an SLP are passive distributors of signals and do not perform any computation or learning.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Sorting Office

Imagine a post office where letters must be sorted into two boxes: **"Local Mail"** and **"Outstation Mail"**.

- Each letter carries certain features: weight, size, distance marker, stamp color, etc. → These are your **inputs ($x_i$)**.
- The postmaster assigns an **importance score** to each feature → These are your **weights ($w_i$)**.
- Sometimes, a base threshold exists even before checking the features → This is the **bias ($b$)**.
- A rule says: *If the total weighted score crosses a cutoff, place in "Outstation"; otherwise, "Local"* → This is the **activation function ($\phi$)**.

Just like the postmaster, the perceptron looks at weighted evidence and makes a binary decision. If the postmaster makes a wrong decision (letter placed in the wrong box), the importance scores (weights) are slightly adjusted based on the error — this is **learning**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the perceptron matter in KTU 2024?**
> It is the *conceptual grandparent* of every modern neural network, including Deep Learning and Transformers. Understanding the SLP rigorously is mandatory before studying Multilayer Perceptrons, Backpropagation, and Support Vector Machines.

## 1.3 Historical Context & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| **Learning Rate ($\eta$)** | $0 < \eta \leq 1$ (typically 0.1 to 1.0) |
| **Activation Function** | Step / Sign / Bipolar Sigmoid |
| **Convergence Criterion** | Zero classification error OR max epochs reached |
| **Number of Layers** | 1 (output) + 1 (input, non-computational) |
| **Decision Boundary** | A single **hyperplane** in $\mathbb{R}^n$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **perceptron criterion (loss)** used in the original Rosenblatt algorithm is fundamentally different from modern loss functions. It only penalizes *misclassified* samples and ignores correctly classified ones entirely.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture Breakdown — Building Blocks

The Single Layer Perceptron has **five operational building blocks**. Each plays a distinct role:

1. **Input Layer ($\vec{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$)**
   - A vector of $n$ features: $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$.
   - It does not compute anything; it merely forwards the signal.

2. **Weight Vector ($\vec{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n$)**
   - Each input $x_i$ is multiplied by a corresponding weight $w_i$.
   - Weights are the **learnable parameters** of the model.

3. **Net Summation Function ($u$)**
   - Computes the linear combination of weighted inputs and the bias.
   - $u = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$

4. **Activation / Transfer Function ($\phi$)**
   - Maps the unbounded net input $u$ to a bounded output range.
   - Common choices: **Step**, **Sign**, **Bipolar Step**, **Sigmoid**.

5. **Output ($y$)**
   - The final prediction: $y = \phi(u)$.

## 2.2 Activation Functions Used in SLP

| Function | Mathematical Form | Output Range | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unipolar Step** | $\phi(u) = \begin{cases} 1 & u \geq 0 \\ 0 & u < 0 \end{cases}$ | $\{0, 1\}$ | Binary classification (0/1) |
| **Bipolar Step / Sign** | $\phi(u) = \begin{cases} +1 & u \geq 0 \\ -1 & u < 0 \end{cases}$ | $\{-1, +1\}$ | Symmetric class labels |
| **Sigmoid (Logistic)** | $\phi(u) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-u}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | Smooth, differentiable |
| **ReLU** | $\phi(u) = \max(0, u)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Rare in classical SLP |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 examinations, the **unipolar step** and **bipolar sign** functions are most frequently tested, as they are tied directly to Rosenblatt's original perceptron convergence theorem.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Net Input** | $u = \vec{w}^T \vec{x} + b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$ | Linear aggregation |
| 2 | **Output (Step)** | $y = \phi(u) \in \{0, 1\}$ or $\{-1, +1\}$ | Prediction |
| 3 | **Weight Update** | $w_i^{(\text{new})} = w_i^{(\text{old})} + \eta \, (t - y) \, x_i$ | Learning rule |
| 4 | **Bias Update** | $b^{(\text{new})} = b^{(\text{old})} + \eta \, (t - y)$ | Bias learning |
| 5 | **Perceptron Criterion Loss** | $E_p = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} (t_i - y_i) \, u_i$ | Penalized over misclassified $\mathcal{M}$ |
| 6 | **Convergence Theorem** | If data is linearly separable, finite iterations $\Rightarrow$ convergence | Guarantees |
| 7 | **Decision Boundary** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = 0$ | $(n-1)$-dimensional hyperplane |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note carefully: In the weight update formula, $(t - y)$ represents the **error signal**, $t$ is the target/true label, and $y$ is the predicted output. If the prediction is correct, the update is zero and weights remain unchanged.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Single Layer Perceptrons, despite their simplicity, are deployed in:

- **Real-time embedded control systems** for binary on/off decisions (e.g., relay triggering, threshold alarms in industrial PLCs).
- **Classical pattern classification** tasks that are fundamentally *linearly separable*: spam filtering (rudimentary), defect detection on assembly lines, and basic medical screening.
- **Historical foundation** for adaptive linear neurons (ADALINE) and modern deep learning.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Limitation:** The SLP **cannot solve the XOR problem** (Minsky & Papert, 1969). It is restricted to problems that are *linearly separable*. This limitation motivated the development of the **Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)** with hidden layers and backpropagation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Perceptron Learning Rule (Rosenblatt, 1958)

The perceptron learning rule is derived from a simple **gradient-inspired update**. We define the perceptron criterion (loss) for a misclassified sample $p$ as:

$$E_p = -u_p \cdot (t_p - y_p)$$

where $u_p = \vec{w}^T \vec{x}_p + b$ is the net input and $t_p - y_p \in \{-1, +1\}$ is the classification error. The total loss is summed over all misclassified samples $\mathcal{M}$:

$$E = -\sum_{p \in \mathcal{M}} u_p (t_p - y_p)$$

**Step 1: Compute the gradient with respect to weight $w_i$.**

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_i} = -\sum_{p \in \mathcal{M}} (t_p - y_p) \frac{\partial u_p}{\partial w_i}$$

**Step 2: Differentiate the net input with respect to $w_i$.**

Since $u_p = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_{p,i} + b$, the partial derivative is:

$$\frac{\partial u_p}{\partial w_i} = x_{p,i}$$

**Step 3: Substitute back into the gradient.**

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_i} = -\sum_{p \in \mathcal{M}} (t_p - y_p) \, x_{p,i}$$

**Step 4: Apply the standard gradient descent update rule.**

Using $w_i^{\text{new}} = w_i^{\text{old}} - \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_i}$:

$$w_i^{\text{new}} = w_i^{\text{old}} + \eta \sum_{p \in \mathcal{M}} (t_p - y_p) \, x_{p,i}$$

**Step 5: Simplify to the online (per-sample) update rule.**

In *online* or *stochastic* mode, the weight is updated sample-by-sample:

$$w_i^{\text{new}} = w_i^{\text{old}} + \eta (t - y) x_i$$

**Step 6: Derive the corresponding bias update.**

Since the bias is the coefficient of a constant input of 1, treating $x_0 = 1$ and $w_0 = b$:

$$b^{\text{new}} = b^{\text{old}} + \eta (t - y)$$

**Step 7: Derive the geometric interpretation of the update.**

The update $\Delta \vec{w} = \eta (t - y) \vec{x}$ moves the weight vector in the **direction of the input** if misclassified. If $(t - y) = +1$ (false negative), the weight vector is **pushed toward** the input; if $(t - y) = -1$ (false positive), it is **pushed away**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 3-Epoch Training Trace

Consider a 2-input bipolar perceptron with **initial weights** $w_1 = 0.3$, $w_2 = -0.1$, **bias** $b = 0.2$, and **learning rate** $\eta = 1.0$. Use a **bipolar step** activation.

Training samples (with bipolar targets):

| Sample | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Target $t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | +1 |
| 2 | 1 | -1 | -1 |
| 3 | -1 | 1 | -1 |
| 4 | -1 | -1 | -1 |

### Epoch 1

**Sample 1** — $(x_1, x_2, t) = (1, 1, +1)$:

$$u = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = (0.3)(1) + (-0.1)(1) + 0.2 = 0.4$$

Since $u = 0.4 \geq 0$, the predicted output is $y = +1$. The error $(t - y) = 0$, so **no update**.

**Sample 2** — $(1, -1, -1)$:

$$u = (0.3)(1) + (-0.1)(-1) + 0.2 = 0.3 + 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.6$$

Since $u = 0.6 \geq 0$, predicted $y = +1$. But target $t = -1$. Error $= -2$.

$$w_1^{\text{new}} = 0.3 + 1.0 \cdot (-2) \cdot 1 = -1.7$$
$$w_2^{\text{new}} = -0.1 + 1.0 \cdot (-2) \cdot (-1) = 1.9$$
$$b^{\text{new}} = 0.2 + 1.0 \cdot (-2) = -1.8$$

**Sample 3** — $(-1, 1, -1)$ with updated weights:

$$u = (-1.7)(-1) + (1.9)(1) + (-1.8) = 1.7 + 1.9 - 1.8 = 1.8$$

Predicted $y = +1$, but $t = -1$. Error $= -2$.

$$w_1^{\text{new}} = -1.7 + (-2)(-1) = 0.3$$
$$w_2^{\text{new}} = 1.9 + (-2)(1) = -0.1$$
$$b^{\text{new}} = -1.8 + (-2) = -3.8$$

**Sample 4** — $(-1, -1, -1)$:

$$u = (0.3)(-1) + (-0.1)(-1) + (-3.8) = -0.3 + 0.1 - 3.8 = -4.0$$

Predicted $y = -1$, target $t = -1$. Correct! **No update.**

End of Epoch 1: $w_1 = 0.3, w_2 = -0.1, b = -3.8$.

### Epoch 2

**Sample 1** — $(1, 1, +1)$:

$$u = 0.3 + (-0.1) + (-3.8) = -3.6 \Rightarrow y = -1$$

Error $= +1 - (-1) = +2$.

$$w_1 = 0.3 + 2 \cdot 1 = 2.3, \quad w_2 = -0.1 + 2 \cdot 1 = 1.9, \quad b = -3.8 + 2 = -1.8$$

**Sample 2** — $(1, -1, -1)$:

$$u = 2.3 + 1.9 \cdot (-1) + (-1.8) = 2.3 - 1.9 - 1.8 = -1.4 \Rightarrow y = -1$$

Target $t = -1$. Correct! No update.

**Sample 3** — $(-1, 1, -1)$:

$$u = (2.3)(-1) + (1.9)(1) + (-1.8) = -2.3 + 1.9 - 1.8 = -2.2 \Rightarrow y = -1$$

Correct! No update.

**Sample 4** — $(-1, -1, -1)$:

$$u = (2.3)(-1) + (1.9)(-1) + (-1.8) = -2.3 - 1.9 - 1.8 = -6.0 \Rightarrow y = -1$$

Correct! No update.

End of Epoch 2: $w_1 = 2.3, w_2 = 1.9, b = -1.8$.

### Epoch 3

**Sample 1** — $(1, 1, +1)$:

$$u = 2.3 + 1.9 - 1.8 = 2.4 \Rightarrow y = +1 \quad \text{Correct!}$$

**Sample 2** — $(1, -1, -1)$:

$$u = 2.3 - 1.9 - 1.8 = -1.4 \Rightarrow y = -1 \quad \text{Correct!}$$

**Sample 3** — $(-1, 1, -1)$:

$$u = -2.3 + 1.9 - 1.8 = -2.2 \Rightarrow y = -1 \quad \text{Correct!}$$

**Sample 4** — $(-1, -1, -1)$:

$$u = -2.3 - 1.9 - 1.8 = -6.0 \Rightarrow y = -1 \quad \text{Correct!}$$

**All samples correctly classified — Convergence achieved at the end of Epoch 3!**

The final decision boundary is:

$$2.3 \, x_1 + 1.9 \, x_2 - 1.8 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = \frac{1.8 - 2.3 x_1}{1.9}$$

## 3.3 Perceptron Convergence Theorem — Statement & Proof Sketch

**Theorem (Rosenblatt, 1958):** If the training data is **linearly separable**, the perceptron learning algorithm is guaranteed to converge to a separating hyperplane in a **finite number of steps**, regardless of the initial weights and the choice of learning rate $\eta > 0$.

**Proof Sketch (using the Novikoff / Block formulation):**

Define a solution weight vector $\vec{w}^*$ such that $t_p (\vec{w}^{*T} \vec{x}_p) > \gamma$ for all $p$ (separability margin $\gamma > 0$). After $k$ weight updates, the squared norm of $\vec{w}_k$ is bounded by $k$, and the inner product $\vec{w}_k^T \vec{w}^*$ grows linearly with $k$. Combining these bounds forces $k$ to be finite. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> The bound on the number of updates is roughly $k \leq \frac{R^2}{\gamma^2}$, where $R$ is the radius bounding the input vectors.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — SLP Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1["X1"] --> sumNode["Summation Unit"]
    input2["X2"] --> sumNode
    input3["X3"] --> sumNode
    inputN["Xn"] --> sumNode
    weight1["W1"] -.-> sumNode
    weight2["W2"] -.-> sumNode
    weight3["W3"] -.-> sumNode
    weightN["Wn"] -.-> sumNode
    bias["Bias b"] -.-> sumNode
    sumNode --> netInput["Net Input u = sum Wi Xi + b"]
    netInput --> activation["Activation Function phi"]
    activation --> output["Output y"]
    target["Target t"] --> errorCalc["Error e = t minus y"]
    output --> errorCalc
    errorCalc --> updater["Weight Updater delta W = eta e X"]
    updater -.-> weight1
    updater -.-> weight2
    updater -.-> weight3
    updater -.-> weightN
    updater -.-> bias
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Perceptron Training Algorithm (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA(["Start"]) --> initA["Initialize weights W to 0 and bias b to 0"]
    initA --> setEta["Set learning rate eta between 0 and 1"]
    setEta --> epochLoop["FOR each epoch from 1 to N max"]
    epochLoop --> sampleLoop["FOR each training sample Xp and target tp"]
    sampleLoop --> computeU["Compute net input u = W dot Xp plus b"]
    computeU --> activate["Apply activation phi to get predicted y"]
    activate --> checkErr{"Is t minus y equal to zero?"}
    checkErr -- Yes --> sampleLoop
    checkErr -- No --> updateW["Update Wi = Wi plus eta times t minus y times Xi"]
    updateW --> updateB["Update b = b plus eta times t minus y"]
    updateB --> sampleLoop
    sampleLoop --> epochLoop
    epochLoop --> convergeCheck{"Did all samples classify correctly?"}
    convergeCheck -- Yes --> stopA(["Stop Converged"])
    convergeCheck -- No --> epochLoop
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Concept of Linear Separability

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClassA["Class Positive Plus 1"]
        pt1["P1 origin x1 0 y1 0"]
        pt2["P2 origin x2 0 y2 0"]
    end
    subgraph ClassB["Class Negative Minus 1"]
        pt3["P3 origin x3 0 y3 0"]
        pt4["P4 origin x4 0 y4 0"]
    end
    hyperplane["Separating Hyperplane W dot X plus b equals 0"] -.-> pt1
    hyperplane -.-> pt2
    hyperplane -.-> pt3
    hyperplane -.-> pt4
    pt1 -.->|"Class +1 above the plane"| hyperplane
    pt3 -.->|"Class -1 below the plane"| hyperplane
```

> [!NOTE]
> The SLP can only draw **one straight hyperplane** through feature space. If your data needs a curved or multi-region boundary (e.g., XOR), a **Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)** with at least one hidden layer is required.
