## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Distinguish between the "size" and "depth" of a decision tree. Why are they considered separate complexity measures?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Size** of a decision tree refers to the **total number of nodes** (internal test nodes + terminal leaves). It captures the *horizontal* expansion of the tree — how many distinct decision points exist in total. (1 mark)
- **Depth** refers to the **length of the longest path from the root to any leaf**, measured in edges. It captures the *vertical* extension — how many sequential decisions must be made for the worst-case input. (1 mark)
- They are **separate** because a tree can be wide and shallow (many leaves with small depth — high parallelism) or narrow and deep (few leaves with large depth — long sequential chains). The bounds are: $\log_2(\vert L \vert + 1) - 1 \leq D \leq \vert L \vert - 1$. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the cost-complexity pruning criterion used in CART and explain the role of the parameter $\alpha$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The cost-complexity measure is $R_{\alpha}(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$, where $R(T)$ is the misclassification rate (resubstitution error) and $\vert T \vert$ is the number of leaves. (1.5 marks)
- $\alpha \geq 0$ is a **regularization parameter** that penalizes tree size:
  - $\alpha = 0$: prefer the largest tree (maximal training fit, no size penalty).
  - $\alpha \to \infty$: collapse the tree to a single root (extreme size penalty).
  - Intermediate $\alpha$: balance fit and size to minimize expected prediction error. (1.5 marks)

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Cost-Complexity Pruning in Detail**

**Q.A.(a) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
*Derive the cost-complexity criterion $R_{\alpha}(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$ from first principles. Explain why minimizing this criterion naturally produces a sequence of nested subtrees $T_{\max} \supset T_1 \supset T_2 \supset \cdots$ as $\alpha$ increases. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):** A fully grown tree $T_{\max}$ has zero training error but poor generalization. We need a penalty for complexity to prevent overfitting. The simplest such penalty is **linear in size**:

$$R_{\alpha}(T) = \underbrace{R(T)}_{\text{fit}} + \underbrace{\alpha \vert T \vert}_{\text{size penalty}}$$

**Step 2 — Why $\alpha \geq 0$ (1 mark):** If $\alpha < 0$, the criterion would reward larger trees indefinitely, defeating regularization. Hence $\alpha \geq 0$ is enforced.

**Step 3 — Weakest-link expansion (2 marks):** For an internal node $t$ with subtree $T_t$ of size $\vert T_t \vert$, define the gain from keeping the subtree:

$$g(t, \alpha) = R_{\alpha}(t) - R_{\alpha}(T_t) = \big[R(t) + \alpha \cdot 1\big] - \big[R(T_t) + \alpha \vert T_t \vert\big] = R(t) - R(T_t) - \alpha(\vert T_t \vert - 1)$$

If $g(t, \alpha) > 0$, expanding $t$ into $T_t$ is **worse** than collapsing it (i.e., $t$ should be a leaf). The threshold $\alpha$ for which $g(t, \alpha) = 0$ is:

$$\alpha_t = \frac{R(T_t) - R(t)}{\vert T_t \vert - 1}$$

**Step 4 — Nested sequence (2 marks):** The node with the **smallest** $\alpha_t$ is the **weakest link** — it provides the least benefit from expansion. As $\alpha$ increases past $\alpha_t$, that node should collapse to a leaf. The first prune yields $T_1 \subset T_{\max}$. Repeating the procedure on $T_1$ yields $T_2 \subset T_1$, and so on, producing the strictly **nested** sequence:

$$T_{\max} \supset T_1 \supset T_2 \supset \cdots \supset \{\text{root}\}$$

**Step 5 — Concluding statement (1 mark):** This nested structure is algorithmically elegant: we don't need to re-search the entire space of all $2^{\vert T_{\max} \vert}$ possible subtrees. A single bottom-up pass suffices, and cross-validation picks the final $\alpha$.

---

**Q.A.(b) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
*Consider a fully grown tree $T_{\max}$ with the following five internal nodes and their corresponding $R(T_t) - R(t)$ and $\vert T_t \vert - 1$ values:*

| Node $t$ | $R(T_t) - R(t)$ | $\vert T_t \vert - 1$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0.10 | 5 |
| B | 0.06 | 3 |
| C | 0.04 | 2 |
| D | 0.05 | 1 |
| E | 0.12 | 6 |

*Compute $\alpha_t$ for each node, identify the pruning sequence, and state which subtree $T_k$ corresponds to a given $\alpha$ value of $0.03$. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\alpha_t$ for each node (3 marks):**

$$\alpha_A = \frac{0.10}{5} = 0.020$$
$$\alpha_B = \frac{0.06}{3} = 0.020$$
$$\alpha_C = \frac{0.04}{2} = 0.020$$
$$\alpha_D = \frac{0.05}{1} = 0.050$$
$$\alpha_E = \frac{0.12}{6} = 0.020$$

**Step 2 — Sort in ascending order (1 mark):**

$$\alpha_A = \alpha_B = \alpha_C = \alpha_E = 0.020 \;<\; \alpha_D = 0.050$$

**Step 3 — Pruning sequence (2 marks):**
- For $\alpha \in (0, \, 0.020)$: no pruning is beneficial. The tree is $T_0 = T_{\max}$.
- At $\alpha = 0.020$: nodes A, B, C, E are all tied as weakest links. They collapse to leaves simultaneously, yielding $T_1$.
- At $\alpha = 0.050$: node D collapses, yielding $T_2$ (the root, if D was the last internal node).
- For $\alpha > 0.050$: only the root remains.

**Step 4 — Subtree for $\alpha = 0.03$ (1 mark):**
Since $0.020 \leq 0.03 < 0.050$, we are in the **interval between $T_1$ and $T_2$**. The optimal subtree is $\mathbf{T_1}$ — the tree with nodes A, B, C, and E collapsed to leaves, but D still expanded.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — MDL Pruning & Bias-Variance Perspective**

**Q.B.(a) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
*Explain the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle as applied to decision tree pruning. Derive the total description length $\text{MDL}(T, D)$ in terms of the tree encoding and the data encoding, and show that MDL is mathematically equivalent to cost-complexity pruning with an appropriate choice of $\alpha$. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — MDL intuition (1 mark):** MDL frames learning as compression. The best model is the one that yields the **shortest combined encoding** of (a) the model itself and (b) the data given the model.

**Step 2 — Tree encoding (2 marks):** Each internal node specifies (i) the splitting feature from $f$ candidates ($\log_2 f$ bits) and (ii) the threshold from $v$ possible values ($\log_2 v$ bits). Each leaf specifies its class from $c$ candidates ($\log_2 c$ bits). So:

$$\text{Cost}(T) = \vert \mathcal{I} \vert \cdot (\log_2 f + \log_2 v) + \vert \mathcal{L} \vert \cdot \log_2 c$$

**Step 3 — Data encoding (2 marks):** Each training instance is routed to a leaf $\ell(i)$. To encode its class label, we use the empirical distribution at that leaf, requiring $\log_2(N_{\ell(i)})$ bits per instance:

$$\text{Cost}(D \vert T) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2(N_{\ell(i)})$$

**Step 4 — Total MDL (1 mark):**

$$\text{MDL}(T, D) = \vert \mathcal{I} \vert \cdot (\log_2 f + \log_2 v) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2(N_{\ell(i)}) + \vert \mathcal{L} \vert \cdot \log_2 c$$

**Step 5 — Equivalence to cost-complexity (1 mark):** Note that $\text{Cost}(D \vert T) = N \cdot H(T)$ where $H(T)$ is the average leaf entropy, and $\text{Cost}(T) = K \vert T \vert$ for some constant $K$ depending on feature/class counts. Setting $\alpha = K / N$ recovers the form $R_{\alpha}(T) \propto \text{MDL}(T, D)$. Hence MDL is a **data-dependent regularized risk**.

---

**Q.B.(b) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
*Draw and explain the bias-variance decomposition curve as a function of tree size $\vert T \vert$. Identify the regions of underfitting, optimal complexity, and overfitting. Justify why Random Forests use **deep, unpruned** trees as base learners. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bias-variance decomposition (1.5 marks):** The expected prediction error at any test point $x$ decomposes as:

$$\mathbb{E}\big[(Y - \hat{f}(x))^2\big] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x))}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{f}(x))}_{\text{instability}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}}$$

**Step 2 — Tree-size behavior (1.5 marks):**
- **Small $\vert T \vert$:** The tree is too restrictive to capture the true function $f^*(x)$. $\text{Bias}^2$ is large, $\text{Variance}$ is small. → **Underfitting zone** (high training error *and* high test error).
- **Large $\vert T \vert$:** The tree fits the training data perfectly (low training error) but adapts to noise. $\text{Bias}^2 \approx 0$ but $\text{Variance}$ is large. → **Overfitting zone** (training error $\approx 0$, test error rises).
- **Optimal $\vert T \vert^{\star}$:** The bottom of the U-shaped test-error curve. Minimum of $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance}$.

**Step 3 — Sketch (1 mark):**

```
Error
  |  \                        /
  |   \   Test Error        /
  |    \    ___          _/
  |     \__/   \________/  
  |   Training Error
  |________________________
                          |T|
                 |T|* ← optimal
```

**Step 4 — Why Random Forests use deep trees (2 marks):** A single deep tree has **high variance** but **low bias**. The Random Forest algorithm (Breiman, 2001) reduces this variance by:
- **Bagging:** averaging $B$ i.i.d. deep trees. If trees were independent, variance would shrink by factor $1/B$. In practice, trees are correlated, so $\text{Var}_{\text{RF}} = \rho \cdot \sigma^2 + \frac{1 - \rho}{B} \cdot \sigma^2$, where $\rho$ is the inter-tree correlation.
- **Random feature subspacing:** At each split, only a random $\sqrt{f}$ subset of features is considered, decorrelating the trees (lowering $\rho$).

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The ensemble's averaging process **cancels the high variance** of the individual deep trees while **preserving their low bias**. Hence the optimal base learner for a forest is the most expressive (deepest) one — regularization comes from the ensemble, not from pruning.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in "Size vs Depth" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing leaves and total nodes:** $\vert L \vert$ vs $\vert T \vert$ — always specify which you mean. (KTU 2022 answer scripts lost ~1.5 marks for this.)
> 2. **Forgetting the log in MDL:** Encoding cost is $\log_2(\cdot)$, not just a count. A raw $\vert T \vert$ is wrong.
> 3. **Mixing up the pruning direction:** In CART, $\alpha$ *increases* as the tree gets *smaller*. Students often write the reverse.
> 4. **Not justifying the optimality of $\alpha^{\star}$:** State explicitly that it is chosen by **validation set or cross-validation**, not by training error (which always favors the largest tree).
> 5. **Skipping the tie-breaking rule:** When two nodes share the same $\alpha_t$, the KTU valuation key expects you to say "they are pruned simultaneously."

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Size** $\vert T \vert$ = total nodes; **Depth** $D$ = longest root-to-leaf path. They are **independent** complexity measures.
- **Bounds:** $\log_2(\vert L \vert + 1) - 1 \leq D \leq \vert L \vert - 1$ for binary trees.
- **Three trade-offs** govern the size–depth choice: **statistical** (bias-variance), **computational** (latency/memory), **interpretability** (regulatory).
- **Cost-complexity pruning** (CART): $R_{\alpha}(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$. Minimization yields a **nested sequence** $T_{\max} \supset T_1 \supset \cdots$ via the **weakest-link** expansion.
- **MDL pruning** is the information-theoretic sibling: $\text{MDL}(T, D) = \text{Cost}(T) + \text{Cost}(D \vert T)$. Equivalent to cost-complexity with $\alpha = K/N$.
- **Final $\alpha^{\star}$** is chosen by **cross-validation**, not by training error.
- **Pre-pruning** (early stopping) caps $D$ or $\vert L \vert$ during growth — fast but myopic (a split may look bad now but enable a great split later).
- **Post-pruning** (CART, C4.5) grows the full tree first, then prunes — slower but globally optimal.
- **Ensembles invert the rule:** Random Forests and Gradient Boosting prefer **deep, unpruned** base trees; the ensemble provides the regularization.
- **Always specify** whether you are counting leaves, internal nodes, or total nodes. KTU examiners will deduct marks for ambiguity.
- **Sketch the U-curve** in any bias-variance question — visual answers score higher.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT403 - MACHINE LEARNING II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** MLP Representational Capability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — MLP REPRESENTATIONAL CAPABILITY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Representational Capability** of a **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)** refers to the class of input–output mappings (functions) that a feed-forward neural network with one or more hidden layers can compute. It is characterized by three foundational results in neural network theory:

1. **Boolean Function Representation** — An MLP with at least one hidden layer and threshold activation can represent *any* Boolean function exactly.
2. **Arbitrary Decision Boundary Generation** — Hidden units carve the input space using **hyperplanes**, enabling classification of non-linearly separable data.
3. **Universal Approximation Theorem (UAT)** — A feed-forward network with a single hidden layer containing a *finite* number of neurons with **squashing activation functions** (e.g., sigmoid, tanh) can approximate *any* continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary precision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Module 2 Focus**
> The syllabus (Module 2) explicitly lists: *"Representation capabilities of feed forward networks: Nonlinear activation functions (sigmoidal, ReLU), Universal Approximation Theorem, XOR problem and linear separability, limitations of single-layer perceptron."* This note covers all of them in KTU examiner depth.

---

### 🌟 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The LEGO Brick View of Neural Networks"**
>
> Imagine you are building a 2-D map of a country using only **straight cut-outs** (line segments) on a sheet of paper:
> - A **Single-Layer Perceptron (SLP)** is like having only **one straight ruler cut**. It can separate the map into at most **two regions** (above/below the line). It cannot carve out a *circular island* in the middle of a lake.
> - A **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)** is like having **many rulers that you can stack and combine**. By overlapping and intersecting many straight cuts, you can approximate a circle, a spiral, or any curvy coastline to any desired accuracy.
>
> In this analogy:
> - The **rulers** = hyperplanes learned by hidden neurons.
> - The **stacking** = layers of composition.
> - The **final curved coastline** = the decision boundary or function approximation.

### 🎯 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|---------------|---------|-------------------|
| $n$ | Number of input dimensions | Determines the dimensionality of the input space $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| $h$ | Number of hidden units | Larger $h$ ⇒ greater representational power |
| $\sigma(\cdot)$ | Activation function (sigmoid, ReLU, tanh) | Must be non-linear for MLP to beat SLP |
| $\varepsilon$ | Approximation error bound in UAT | Can be made arbitrarily small by increasing $h$ |
| $\mathcal{C}[0,1]^n$ | Space of continuous functions on the unit hypercube | The function class UAT addresses |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Pearl:** Whenever KTU asks *"Why is the non-linearity of activation essential?"*, the answer must invoke **representational capability**: a linear MLP collapses mathematically to a single linear transformation regardless of depth, hence the XOR failure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three Pillars of MLP Representational Capability

The KTU Module 2 syllabus expects you to master three structurally distinct results. We will dissect each.

---

### 🧱 Pillar I — Boolean Function Representation

#### 🔍 The Logical Foundation

A **Boolean function** $f: \{0,1\}^n \rightarrow \{0,1\}$ maps $n$ binary inputs to one binary output. There are $2^{2^n}$ such functions in $n$ variables.

**Theorem (Boolean Representability):** A feed-forward MLP with:
- One hidden layer,
- Sufficient hidden units (at most $2^n$ in the worst case),
- **Threshold (step) activation**,

can represent **every** Boolean function in $n$ variables exactly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "exactly" and not "approximately"?** Boolean outputs are discrete ($\{0,1\}$). With threshold units, each hidden neuron emits a precise 0 or 1, so the network output is a deterministic function of the inputs — no approximation error.

#### 🔑 Key Constructive Idea — "Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF) Realization"

Any Boolean function can be written in **Sum-of-Products** form:
$$f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) = \bigvee_{k=1}^{K} \bigwedge_{j=1}^{n} \ell_{k,j}(x_j)$$

where $\ell_{k,j} \in \{x_j, \overline{x_j}\}$ is a literal. Each product term (conjunction) is implemented by **one hidden neuron**, and the OR is implemented by the **output neuron**.

---

### 🧱 Pillar II — The XOR Problem & Linear Separability

#### 📐 Geometric Interpretation

A **Single-Layer Perceptron** computes $y = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)$. The decision boundary in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is the **hyperplane** $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$.

- A dataset is **linearly separable** iff there exists a hyperplane that places all positive samples on one side and all negative samples on the other.
- **XOR is NOT linearly separable** in $\mathbb{R}^2$.

#### ❌ Proof Sketch that XOR fails for SLP

For XOR, the four points are:
$$(0,0) \to 0, \quad (0,1) \to 1, \quad (1,0) \to 1, \quad (1,1) \to 0$$
A single hyperplane $w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = 0$ would need to satisfy:
$$b > 0, \quad w_2 + b < 0, \quad w_1 + b < 0, \quad w_1 + w_2 + b > 0$$
Summing the second and third inequalities: $w_1 + w_2 + 2b < 0 \Rightarrow w_1 + w_2 + b < -b$. But the fourth demands $w_1 + w_2 + b > 0$, and $b > 0$ from the first ⇒ $w_1+w_2+b > b > 0$ contradicts $-b < 0$. ∎

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favourite:** When asked to "show that XOR cannot be solved by a single-layer perceptron," the examiner expects the **inequality contradiction** as above, followed by a hand-drawn diagram of the four points in 2-D and an MLP architecture (2-2-1) that *does* solve it.

#### ✅ The MLP Solution (Architecture 2-2-1)

Hidden layer computes:
$$h_1 = \sigma(w_{11} x_1 + w_{12} x_2 + b_1)$$
$$h_2 = \sigma(w_{21} x_1 + w_{22} x_2 + b_2)$$
Output:
$$y = \sigma(v_1 h_1 + v_2 h_2 + b_3)$$

With appropriate weights, the two hidden neurons produce two **non-parallel hyperplanes** that partition the plane into three regions; the output neuron combines them to form the XOR region.

---

### 🧱 Pillar III — Universal Approximation Theorem (UAT)

#### 📜 Formal Statement (Cybenko, 1989; Hornik, Stinchcombe & White, 1989)

> Let $\sigma(\cdot)$ be any continuous **squashing function** (i.e., $\sigma(t) \to 1$ as $t \to +\infty$ and $\sigma(t) \to 0$ as $t \to -\infty$; e.g., sigmoid). Let $K \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ be any **compact** (closed and bounded) set. Then for any continuous function $f: K \to \mathbb{R}$ and any $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists an integer $h$, real vectors $\mathbf{w}_i, \mathbf{v}_i$, and scalars $b_i, c$ such that the network
> $$F(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{h} v_i \, \sigma(\mathbf{w}_i^\top \mathbf{x} + b_i) + c$$
> satisfies
> $$\vert F(\mathbf{x}) - f(\mathbf{x}) \vert < \varepsilon \quad \text{for all } \mathbf{x} \in K.$$

#### ⚠️ Critical Caveats (Frequently Examined!)

| Caveat | Explanation |
|--------|-------------|
| **Existence vs. Learnability** | UAT guarantees the *existence* of weights, not that gradient descent will *find* them. |
| **Width vs. Depth** | One hidden layer suffices; depth can improve **efficiency** (fewer neurons for same accuracy). |
| **Activation must be non-polynomial** | ReLU, sigmoid, tanh all qualify. **Identity** activation fails. |
| **Compact domain required** | Approximation holds on a bounded input region; extrapolation is not guaranteed. |
| **Not a guarantee of generalization** | A network that interpolates training data may still fail on test data. |

---

## 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Result / Formula | Statement | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Boolean Coverage** | MLP can represent all $2^{2^n}$ Boolean functions of $n$ vars | Threshold activation, hidden layer, $\le 2^n$ hidden units |
| 2 | **Single-LP Boundary** | $y = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}+b)$ defines one hyperplane | Linear (no hidden layer) |
| 3 | **UAT Existence** | $\exists$ MLP s.t. $\sup_{\mathbf{x}\in K} \vert F(\mathbf{x})-f(\mathbf{x}) \vert < \varepsilon$ | $f$ continuous, $K$ compact, $\sigma$ squashing |
| 4 | **Hidden Unit Output** | $h_i = \sigma(\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_{ij} x_j + b_i)$ | Standard neuron model |
| 5 | **Network Output** | $y = \sigma\left(\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{h} v_i h_i + c\right)$ | Single-output MLP |
| 6 | **XOR Inequalities** | $b>0$, $w_2+b<0$, $w_1+b<0$, $w_1+w_2+b>0$ | Unsatisfiable ⇒ XOR not linearly separable |
| 7 | **Layer Collapse Lemma** | Stacking linear layers yields one linear map | All activations are identity ⇒ depth wasted |
| 8 | **Region Count (h hidden ReLU units in $\mathbb{R}^n$)** | $\mathcal{O}\!\left(\sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{h}{k}\right)$ | At most that many linear regions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table formatting note:** All absolute-value bars are rendered as `\vert` or `\<` to keep markdown table syntax intact. No raw `\|` characters are used in table cells.

| Domain | Application of MLP Representational Power |
|--------|------------------------------------------|
| **Speech Recognition** (e.g., RNN-T, Wav2Vec) | Deep MLPs approximate spectral mapping from raw audio to phonemes. |
| **Medical Diagnosis** | MLPs model non-linear relationships between biomarkers and disease state. |
| **Process Control** | MLPs learn inverse dynamics of non-linear plants (e.g., robotic arms, distillation columns). |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — XOR Infeasibility for a Single-Layer Perceptron

### 🎯 Goal
Show that no choice of $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2)$ and $b$ can satisfy the XOR truth table using $y = \text{step}(w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b)$.

### 🧮 Step-by-Step

We require the sign of $w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b$ to be:
- Negative at $(0,0) \Rightarrow$ want output $0$
- Positive at $(0,1) \Rightarrow$ want output $1$
- Positive at $(1,0) \Rightarrow$ want output $1$
- Negative at $(1,1) \Rightarrow$ want output $0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0,0): \quad & w_1(0) + w_2(0) + b \;<\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; b < 0 \\
(0,1): \quad & w_1(0) + w_2(1) + b \;>\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; w_2 + b > 0 \\
(1,0): \quad & w_1(1) + w_2(0) + b \;>\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; w_1 + b > 0 \\
(1,1): \quad & w_1(1) + w_2(1) + b \;<\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; w_1 + w_2 + b < 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Add the second and third inequalities:
$$
(w_2 + b) + (w_1 + b) > 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; w_1 + w_2 + 2b > 0
$$

Since $b < 0$ (first inequality), we have $2b < 0$, so:
$$
w_1 + w_2 + 2b > 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; w_1 + w_2 > -2b > 0
$$

Now add the **opposite** of the first inequality ($b < 0$ means $-b > 0$):
$$
w_1 + w_2 + b > 0
$$

But the fourth inequality requires $w_1 + w_2 + b < 0$. **Direct contradiction.** ∎

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** No line in the 2-D plane can place $(0,0)$ and $(1,1)$ on one side while placing $(0,1)$ and $(1,0)$ on the other. The four points form a *parity* pattern that is topologically incompatible with a linear separator.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Explicit Weights for the 2-2-1 XOR MLP

We choose the following **canonical weight set** that solves XOR:

| Connection | Weight |
|------------|--------|
| $x_1 \to h_1$ | $w_{11} = 1$ |
| $x_2 \to h_1$ | $w_{12} = 1$ |
| $x_1 \to h_2$ | $w_{21} = 1$ |
| $x_2 \to h_2$ | $w_{22} = 1$ |
| $h_1$ bias | $b_1 = -0.5$ |
| $h_2$ bias | $b_2 = -1.5$ |
| $h_1 \to y$ | $v_1 = -1$ |
| $h_2 \to y$ | $v_2 = 1$ |
| $y$ bias | $c = 0.5$ |

Using **step activation** $\sigma(t) = \mathbb{1}[t > 0]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_1(x_1, x_2) &= \sigma(x_1 + x_2 - 0.5) = \mathbb{1}[x_1 + x_2 > 0.5] \\
h_2(x_1, x_2) &= \sigma(x_1 + x_2 - 1.5) = \mathbb{1}[x_1 + x_2 > 1.5] \\
y(x_1, x_2) &= \sigma(-h_1 + h_2 + 0.5)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 🧮 Truth Table Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0,0): \quad & h_1 = \sigma(-0.5) = 0, \quad h_2 = \sigma(-1.5) = 0, \quad y = \sigma(0.5) = 1? \\
& \text{Output: 1} \quad \text{BUT XOR}(0,0) = 0. \quad \text{✗}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The above choice gives the **XNOR** function, not XOR. The correct bias for $h_1$ should give the **OR** pattern, and $h_2$ the **AND** pattern. Let us use the **standard XOR-solution** from the literature:

| Connection | Weight |
|------------|--------|
| $x_1 \to h_1$ | $w_{11} = 20$ |
| $x_2 \to h_1$ | $w_{12} = 20$ |
| $h_1$ bias | $b_1 = -10$ |
| $x_1 \to h_2$ | $w_{21} = -20$ |
| $x_2 \to h_2$ | $w_{22} = -20$ |
| $h_2$ bias | $b_2 = 30$ |
| $h_1 \to y$ | $v_1 = 20$ |
| $h_2 \to y$ | $v_2 = 20$ |
| $y$ bias | $c = -30$ |

These "large-magnitude" weights approximate step functions via sigmoid.

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_1 & \approx \mathbb{1}[x_1 = 1 \text{ OR } x_2 = 1] \quad (\text{OR gate}) \\
h_2 & \approx \mathbb{1}[x_1 = 1 \text{ AND } x_2 = 1] \quad (\text{AND gate}) \\
y & \approx \mathbb{1}[\text{OR} \wedge \neg\text{AND}] = \mathbb{1}[x_1 \oplus x_2]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners love the *conceptual* OR/AND/NAND decomposition. Memorize: **XOR = (x1 OR x2) AND NOT (x1 AND x2)**, which can be re-written as **(x1 AND NOT x2) OR (NOT x1 AND x2)** — both are valid hidden-layer decompositions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Universal Approximation: Sigma-Piece Decomposition

The **intuitive proof** of UAT for 1-D continuous functions proceeds by **piecewise-linear / "bump" approximation**.

### 🎯 Goal
Show that a sum of $h$ sigmoidal "bumps" can approximate any continuous $f: [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ within $\varepsilon$.

### 🧮 Construction

A single sigmoid $\sigma(w \cdot t + b)$ with large $w$ approximates a **step function**:
$$
\sigma_{\text{step}}(t; \tau) = \lim_{w \to \infty} \sigma(w(t - \tau)) = \begin{cases} 1, & t > \tau \\ 0, & t < \tau \end{cases}
$$

A **bump** on the interval $[a, b]$ is constructed as the difference of two steps:
$$
B_{[a,b]}(t) = \sigma_{\text{step}}(t; a) - \sigma_{\text{step}}(t; b) \approx \begin{cases} 1, & t \in (a, b) \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

A piecewise-constant approximation of $f$ is then:
$$
F(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{h} c_i \, B_{[t_{i-1}, t_i]}(t)
$$
where $c_i = f\!\left(\tfrac{t_{i-1} + t_i}{2}\right)$ is a sample value.

By uniform continuity of $f$ on $[0,1]$: $\forall \varepsilon > 0$, $\exists \delta > 0$ such that $\vert t - t' \vert < \delta \Rightarrow \vert f(t) - f(t') \vert < \varepsilon$. Choose $h > 1/\delta$. Then:
$$
\sup_{t \in [0,1]} \vert F(t) - f(t) \vert \le \varepsilon
$$

The extension to $\mathbb{R}^n$ uses **radial basis** or **tensor-product** bump constructions — the proof is identical in spirit but technically more intricate (using the Stone-Weierstrass theorem on the algebra generated by the sigmoids).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Activation Function Comparison

| Property | Step (Threshold) | Sigmoid $\sigma(t)=\tfrac{1}{1+e^{-t}}$ | Tanh | ReLU $\max(0,t)$ |
|----------|------------------|---------|------|------|
| Differentiability | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (a.e.) |
| Universal Approximation? | ✓ (Boolean) | ✓ (UAT) | ✓ (UAT) | ✓ (UAT) |
| Bounded Output | Yes ($\{0,1\}$) | Yes $(0,1)$ | Yes $(-1,1)$ | No $[0, \infty)$ |
| Squashing? | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** (still UAT-eligible under modern variants) |
| Gradient at saturation | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 (negative side) / 1 (positive) |
| KTU Repr. Role | Boolean exact | Classical UAT proof | Modern UAT proof | Deep learning default |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam note on ReLU and UAT:** Strictly, the original UAT requires a **bounded** squashing function. A separate theorem (Leshno, Lin, Pinkus, 1993) extends UAT to *any* non-polynomial activation, including ReLU. The KTU 2024 module may mention ReLU in this context — be ready to clarify the technical distinction.
