# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the Universal Approximation Theorem for a feed-forward neural network with a single hidden layer. Mention any two conditions required for the theorem to hold.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Universal Approximation Theorem (Cybenko, 1989) states that a feed-forward neural network with **one hidden layer** containing a **finite number of neurons** with **squashing activation functions** (e.g., sigmoid) can approximate **any continuous function** on a **compact subset** of $\mathbb{R}^n$ to an **arbitrary degree of accuracy** $\varepsilon > 0$.

**Two conditions:**
1. The activation function $\sigma$ must be a continuous, bounded, non-constant, squashing function (e.g., sigmoid, tanh).
2. The domain of the function must be **compact** (closed and bounded), and the target function must be **continuous** on this domain.

(1 mark for theorem statement, ½ mark each for the two conditions)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain why a single-layer perceptron cannot solve the XOR problem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A single-layer perceptron defines a single **linear decision boundary** (hyperplane) in the input space: $y = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x} + b)$. The XOR truth table requires the four points $(0,0), (0,1), (1,0), (1,1)$ to be classified such that $(0,0)$ and $(1,1)$ are on one side, while $(0,1)$ and $(1,0)$ are on the other side. This arrangement is **not linearly separable** because no straight line in 2-D can simultaneously place diagonally opposite points on the same side while the other diagonal pair lies on the opposite side. Mathematically, the four inequalities $b<0$, $w_2+b>0$, $w_1+b>0$, $w_1+w_2+b<0$ are inconsistent (proven by summing them to obtain a contradiction). Hence a single-layer perceptron fails, and a **multi-layer perceptron with at least one hidden layer** is required to learn XOR.

(1 mark for hyperplane concept, 1 mark for geometric reasoning, 1 mark for contradiction/mathematical proof)

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a multi-layer perceptron (MLP). State and explain the **Universal Approximation Theorem**. Discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove that the **XOR function** is not linearly separable by a single-layer perceptron. Then, show how a 2-2-1 MLP with appropriate weights solves the XOR problem. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Architecture of MLP (2 marks):**

An MLP consists of:
- An **input layer** that receives the feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- One or more **hidden layers**, each containing $h_\ell$ neurons computing $h_i^{(\ell)} = \sigma\!\left(\sum_j w_{ij}^{(\ell)} h_j^{(\ell-1)} + b_i^{(\ell)}\right)$.
- An **output layer** that produces the final prediction $y$.

Information flows strictly forward (no cycles), making it a **feed-forward** network.

```
Input Layer      Hidden Layer(s)     Output Layer
  x1 ─────┐        ┌── H1 ──┐
  x2 ─────┼───────►│        │
   ⋮      │        │  ⋮     │───────►  y
  xn ─────┘        └── Hh ──┘
```

**Universal Approximation Theorem (3 marks):**

> *Let $\sigma$ be a continuous squashing activation function. For any continuous function $f : K \to \mathbb{R}$ defined on a compact set $K \subset \mathbb{R}^n$, and any $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a single-hidden-layer MLP $F(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{h} v_i \sigma(\mathbf{w}_i^\top \mathbf{x} + b_i) + c$ such that $\sup_{\mathbf{x} \in K} \vert F(\mathbf{x}) - f(\mathbf{x}) \vert < \varepsilon$.*

**Limitations (2 marks):**

1. **Existence ≠ Learnability:** The theorem guarantees weights exist but does not guarantee gradient-based training will find them.
2. **Width can be impractically large:** The required $h$ may grow exponentially with input dimension.
3. **Compact domain only:** Approximation is not guaranteed outside the training region (poor extrapolation).
4. **No generalization guarantee:** Approximating training data well does not imply good test performance.
5. **Activation restriction:** The original proof requires squashing activations; modern variants (e.g., ReLU) need separate theorems.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Geometric Proof of Non-Separability (3 marks):**

The XOR truth table requires:
- $(0,0) \to 0$, $(1,1) \to 0$ — same class
- $(0,1) \to 1$, $(1,0) \to 1$ — same class

In the 2-D plane, these four points occupy the corners of a unit square. Class-0 points are on the **main diagonal**; class-1 points are on the **anti-diagonal**. A single straight line cannot separate the two diagonals — any line that puts $(0,0)$ and $(1,1)$ together inevitably misclassifies one of $(0,1)$ or $(1,0)$.

**Algebraic Proof (2 marks):**

We require $b < 0$, $w_2 + b > 0$, $w_1 + b > 0$, $w_1 + w_2 + b < 0$. Adding the second and third: $w_1 + w_2 + 2b > 0$. Adding $-b > 0$ to both sides: $w_1 + w_2 + b > 0$, contradicting the fourth inequality. Hence unsatisfiable.

**MLP Solution (2 marks):**

A 2-2-1 MLP uses two hidden neurons to compute OR and AND gates, then combines them in the output neuron to compute XOR = OR $\wedge \neg$AND. With weights (e.g., $w_{11}=w_{12}=20$, $b_1=-10$, $w_{21}=w_{22}=-20$, $b_2=30$, $v_1=v_2=20$, $c=-30$), all four XOR cases are correctly classified (as shown in Section 3.2 above).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** What are the **representational capabilities** of a feed-forward neural network? Explain how an MLP can represent any **Boolean function** in $n$ variables. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the role of **non-linear activation functions** in determining the representational power of MLPs? Show mathematically that a multi-layer network with **linear activations** collapses to a single linear transformation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Three Representational Capabilities (3 marks):**

1. **Boolean Function Representation:** Exact representation of any Boolean function $f: \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$ using threshold activations and a single hidden layer.
2. **Arbitrary Decision Boundaries:** A multi-layer network can generate arbitrarily complex non-linear decision boundaries by combining hyperplanes from hidden neurons.
3. **Universal Approximation:** Approximation of any continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary precision.

**Boolean Function Realization (4 marks):**

Any Boolean function of $n$ variables can be written in **Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF)**:
$$f(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = \bigvee_{k=1}^{K} \bigwedge_{j=1}^{n} \ell_{k,j}(x_j)$$
where $\ell_{k,j} \in \{x_j, \overline{x_j}\}$.

**Construction:**
- Assign **one hidden neuron** to each min-term (product term) in the DNF. The hidden neuron's weights are set to $+w$ for positive literals, $-w$ for negative literals, and bias $-w(n-1) + \theta$ where $\theta$ is a small threshold. The neuron fires (outputs 1) **only** when all literals are satisfied.
- The **output neuron** performs a logical OR over the hidden neurons' outputs (using a threshold activation with a small positive bias).
- The number of hidden units required is at most $2^n$ (worst case, when the function has $2^n$ min-terms). Many functions need far fewer.

For example, **XOR = (x1 $\wedge$ $\overline{x2}$) $\vee$ ($\overline{x1}$ $\wedge$ x2)** uses 2 min-terms ⇒ 2 hidden units.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Role of Non-linearity (3 marks):**

Non-linear activations enable the network to:
1. **Stack layers meaningfully:** Without non-linearity, depth adds no expressive power.
2. **Generate non-linear decision boundaries:** A single hyperplane becomes many hyperplanes in combination.
3. **Approximate arbitrary continuous functions:** As per UAT.
4. **Model multiplicative interactions** between input features.

**Mathematical Collapse Proof (4 marks):**

Consider a 2-layer network with linear activations $\sigma(t) = t$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{h} &= \mathbf{W}_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1 \\
\mathbf{y} &= \mathbf{W}_2 \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $\mathbf{h}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{y} &= \mathbf{W}_2 (\mathbf{W}_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1) + \mathbf{b}_2 \\
&= \underbrace{(\mathbf{W}_2 \mathbf{W}_1)}_{\mathbf{W}_{\text{eff}}} \mathbf{x} + \underbrace{(\mathbf{W}_2 \mathbf{b}_1 + \mathbf{b}_2)}_{\mathbf{b}_{\text{eff}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **mathematically identical to a single linear layer** with combined weights $\mathbf{W}_{\text{eff}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ and combined bias $\mathbf{b}_{\text{eff}} \in \mathbb{R}^m$. By induction, a $L$-layer "deep" linear network collapses to:
$$\mathbf{y} = \left(\prod_{\ell=1}^{L} \mathbf{W}_\ell\right) \mathbf{x} + \text{combined bias}$$
which is still a single affine transformation. Hence, **non-linear activations are necessary** for depth to provide any representational benefit.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones for "MLP Representational Capability" Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Universal Approximation" with "Universal Representation":** UAT says networks can *approximate*, not exactly *represent*, continuous functions. Using the word "exactly" loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Omitting the "squashing" condition:** Stating "any activation" instead of "any non-linear, bounded, squashing activation" is a frequent half-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting the compact-domain requirement:** Many students write UAT as holding "everywhere." It does not.
> 4. **XOR proof — skipping the algebraic contradiction:** A purely verbal "XOR cannot be separated by a line" without the inequality contradiction loses at least 1 of the 3 marks allocated.
> 5. **Writing $\sigma(\cdot)$ as linear in a UAT-related question:** The single biggest reason for full-mark denial in representation theorems.
> 6. **Mixing up architecture diagrams:** Confusing the number of *layers* (counted as weight layers) with the number of *neurons* in a layer. KTU expects the convention: Input layer (no weights) + Hidden layers + Output layer.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ MLP's representational power stems from **three pillars**: Boolean completeness, hyperplane combinations, and UAT.
- ✅ **Linear separability** is the geometric condition for SLP solvability; XOR violates it.
- ✅ **Universal Approximation Theorem** guarantees *existence* of a 1-hidden-layer network approximating any continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary accuracy.
- ✅ UAT requires **squashing (sigmoidal) activations** — ReLU needs the Leshno-Lin-Pinkus extension.
- ✅ **Depth without non-linearity is useless:** Linear MLPs collapse to a single linear map.
- ✅ XOR = (OR) AND (NOT AND) = ($x_1 \wedge \overline{x_2}$) $\vee$ ($\overline{x_1} \wedge x_2$).
- ✅ The 2-2-1 XOR architecture uses OR and AND hidden units feeding into a NAND-like output.
- ✅ **Width vs. Depth trade-off:** A wide single-hidden-layer network can match a deep network in principle; depth is preferred for *efficiency*.
- ✅ **Existence ≠ Learnability:** UAT does not guarantee gradient descent will find the approximating weights.
- ✅ For Boolean functions, the **DNF / sum-of-products** form maps directly onto an MLP architecture.
- ✅ MLP with threshold activations is **universally expressive for Boolean** functions; sigmoid/ReLU variants **approximate** this.
- ✅ KTU Module 2 explicitly tests: linear separability, XOR proof, MLP architecture, activation role, and UAT statement + conditions + caveats.

### 📌 Mnemonic for UAT Conditions

> **"CCS-CC"** — **C**ontinuous activation, **C**ompact domain, **S**quashing function, **C**ontinuous target, plus **Compact** once more for emphasis.

### 📌 Mnemonic for XOR Decomposition

> **"ONE-AND-NOT"** — **O**R $\wedge$ **N**OT(**AND**) gives XOR in one line.

### 🎯 Most Likely KTU Question Types

| Type | Frequency | Marks |
|------|-----------|-------|
| State UAT and conditions | Very High | 3–7 |
| Prove XOR non-separable | Very High | 3–7 |
| MLP architecture diagram + Boolean realization | High | 7–14 |
| Why non-linearity is essential (collapse proof) | Medium | 3–7 |
| Activation function comparison | Medium | 3–7 |
| Limitations of UAT | Medium | 3–5 |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Back Propagation Algorithm: Back Propagation Learning Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT403 - MACHINE LEARNING II
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Back Propagation Algorithm: Back Propagation Learning Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Back Propagation (BP) Learning Law:** A *supervised learning* algorithm used to train multi-layer Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) by propagating the *error signal* from the output layer back through the hidden layers to update the synaptic weights. Mathematically, it is the application of the **gradient-descent optimization rule on the error (loss) surface** with respect to each weight, using the **chain rule of calculus** to compute partial derivatives layer-by-layer in the reverse direction of activation flow.

The Back Propagation Learning Law is formally stated as:

$$ w_{ij}^{new} = w_{ij}^{old} + \Delta w_{ij} $$

where the weight correction $\Delta w_{ij}$ is proportional to the negative gradient of the instantaneous error $E$ with respect to the weight $w_{ij}$:

$$ \Delta w_{ij} = -\eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}} $$

The constant $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (typically $0 < \eta < 1$), which controls the step size during gradient descent.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "The Blame Game in Reverse":**
> Imagine a football team that lost a match **5–0**. The coach (the *back-propagation algorithm*) starts at the goalkeeper (the *output layer*) and asks, *"Who is to blame for the 5 goals conceded?"* The defender (*last hidden layer*) is told, *"You let 3 goals in."* The midfielder (*previous hidden layer*) is told, *"You didn't intercept, leading to 2 of those."* Finally, the striker (*input layer*) is told, *"You lost possession 5 times."* Each player (each *synapse / weight*) is then told exactly *how much* to adjust their play next time (the *weight update*). The error is **propagated backward**, layer by layer, and every connection is updated *proportional to its contribution to the final error*. This is precisely what back propagation does in a neural network.

### 1.3 Historical & Syllabus Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard Architecture:** A **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)** with at least one **hidden layer** trained using the **Generalized Delta Rule (GDR)**.
> - **Activation Function (Standard):** **Sigmoid (Logistic)** function, $f(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$, because of its convenient derivative $f'(x) = f(x)[1 - f(x)]$.
> - **Loss Function:** Mean Squared Error (MSE), $E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k}(d_k - y_k)^2$.
> - **Learning Paradigm:** Supervised, batch (or online/stochastic) gradient descent.
> - **Key KTU Keyword Triad to memorize:** *Gradient Descent + Chain Rule + Generalized Delta Rule.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a Typical BP Network

Consider a feed-forward network with:
- **Input layer** with $n$ neurons (input vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$).
- **One hidden layer** with $h$ neurons (hidden output $\mathbf{h} = [h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_h]^T$).
- **Output layer** with $m$ neurons (output $\mathbf{y} = [y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_m]^T$).
- **Weight matrices:** $\mathbf{W}^{(1)}$ (input → hidden, size $h \times n$) and $\mathbf{W}^{(2)}$ (hidden → output, size $m \times h$).
- **Bias terms:** $\mathbf{b}^{(1)}$ (hidden) and $\mathbf{b}^{(2)}$ (output).

### 2.2 The Two-Phase Operational Concept

The Back Propagation Learning Law operates in **two alternating passes** for every training pattern $(\mathbf{x}^{(p)}, \mathbf{d}^{(p)})$:

**Phase 1 — Forward Pass (Activation Propagation):**
- Input signals flow *left to right* through the network.
- Each neuron computes a *net input* and applies the *activation function* to produce its output.
- No weights are changed during this phase.

**Phase 2 — Backward Pass (Error Propagation & Weight Update):**
- The error $E$ is computed at the output.
- The error signal $\delta$ is *propagated backward* (right to left) through the network.
- Weights are updated using the **Generalized Delta Rule**: $\Delta w = -\eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w}$.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown (The "Why" and the "How")

| Step | Operation | Why it is done | How it is done |
|------|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | Initialize weights randomly (small values, e.g., $\in [-0.5, 0.5]$) | Breaks symmetry; ensures neurons learn distinct features | Random number generator |
| 2 | Present input $\mathbf{x}$ and desired output $\mathbf{d}$ | Supervised learning needs target signal | Read from training set |
| 3 | **Forward pass**: compute $net$ and $y$ for each neuron | To obtain the actual network output | $net_j = \sum_i w_{ij} x_i$, $y_j = f(net_j)$ |
| 4 | Compute output error $E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_k (d_k - y_k)^2$ | Quantifies the discrepancy between prediction and target | Direct substitution |
| 5 | Compute output-layer delta $\delta_k^{(o)} = (d_k - y_k) f'(net_k)$ | Required by chain rule: $\partial E / \partial w = \partial E / \partial y \cdot \partial y / \partial net \cdot \partial net / \partial w$ | BP equation for output |
| 6 | Propagate delta to hidden layer $\delta_j^{(h)} = f'(net_j) \sum_k \delta_k^{(o)} w_{jk}^{(2)}$ | Sends the "blame" backward using chain rule | BP equation for hidden |
| 7 | Update all weights: $w^{new} = w^{old} + \eta \delta \cdot (\text{input to that neuron})$ | Moves weights in the direction of steepest descent on $E$ | Generalized delta rule |
| 8 | Repeat for all patterns; iterate until $E < \epsilon$ or max epochs reached | Drives the network toward a local minimum of $E$ | Outer training loop |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet — Back Propagation Learning Law (Memorize this table)**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Net input at neuron $j$ | $net_j = \sum_{i} w_{ij} x_i + b_j$ | Weighted sum of inputs |
| 2 | Sigmoid activation | $f(net) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-net}}$ | Output $\in (0, 1)$ |
| 3 | Derivative of sigmoid | $f'(net) = f(net)[1 - f(net)] = y(1-y)$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Mean Squared Error (per pattern) | $E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{m}(d_k - y_k)^2$ | Energy / loss function |
| 5 | Output-layer delta (signal) | $\delta_k^{(o)} = (d_k - y_k) \cdot y_k(1 - y_k)$ | Local gradient at output |
| 6 | Hidden-layer delta (signal) | $\delta_j^{(h)} = h_j(1 - h_j) \sum_{k=1}^{m} \delta_k^{(o)} w_{jk}^{(2)}$ | Back-propagated error |
| 7 | Output weight update | $w_{jk}^{(2)}(t+1) = w_{jk}^{(2)}(t) + \eta \cdot \delta_k^{(o)} \cdot h_j$ | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| 8 | Hidden weight update | $w_{ij}^{(1)}(t+1) = w_{ij}^{(1)}(t) + \eta \cdot \delta_j^{(h)} \cdot x_i$ | Same $\eta$ typically |
| 9 | Learning rate bounds | $0 < \eta < 1$ | Typical: $0.1$ to $0.5$ |
| 10 | Weight update with momentum | $\Delta w(t+1) = \eta \delta x + \alpha \Delta w(t)$ | $\alpha$ = momentum coeff. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Back propagation is the foundational training algorithm for nearly all deep learning models — Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs/LSTMs), Transformers — and is used in image recognition, natural language processing, medical diagnosis, autonomous vehicles, financial forecasting, and control systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Exhaustive Derivation of the Back Propagation Learning Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Derive the weight-update equations of the Generalized Delta Rule for a 2-layer feed-forward network (1 hidden layer) using **gradient descent** and the **chain rule**.

---

#### **Step 1: Define the Network Variables**

Let the input vector be $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$. Let the hidden-layer outputs be $\mathbf{h} = (h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_p)$ and the final outputs be $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_m)$. The weight from input neuron $i$ to hidden neuron $j$ is $w_{ij}^{(1)}$; the weight from hidden neuron $j$ to output neuron $k$ is $w_{jk}^{(2)}$.

The net input to hidden neuron $j$ is:

$$
net_j^{(h)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ij}^{(1)} \, x_i
$$

The output of hidden neuron $j$ after sigmoid activation is:

$$
h_j = f\left(net_j^{(h)}\right) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-net_j^{(h)}}}
$$

Similarly, the net input to output neuron $k$ is:

$$
net_k^{(o)} = \sum_{j=1}^{p} w_{jk}^{(2)} \, h_j
$$

And the final output is:

$$
y_k = f\left(net_k^{(o)}\right) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-net_k^{(o)}}}
$$

---

#### **Step 2: Define the Error (Loss) Function**

We use the instantaneous sum-squared error for a single training pattern:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{m} (d_k - y_k)^2
$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is included for mathematical convenience (it cancels the exponent 2 during differentiation).

---

#### **Step 3: Derive the Update Rule for Output-Layer Weights $w_{jk}^{(2)}$**

By the **gradient descent rule**, the change in weight must be proportional to the negative gradient of the error:

$$
\Delta w_{jk}^{(2)} = -\eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}}
$$

Apply the **chain rule of calculus** to expand the partial derivative. Since $E$ depends on $y_k$, which depends on $net_k^{(o)}$, which depends on $w_{jk}^{(2)}$:

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k}{\partial net_k^{(o)}} \cdot \frac{\partial net_k^{(o)}}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}}
$$

Now evaluate each factor:

**(a)** $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial y_k}$:

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y_k}\left[\frac{1}{2}\sum_{k}(d_k - y_k)^2\right] = -(d_k - y_k)
$$

**(b)** $\dfrac{\partial y_k}{\partial net_k^{(o)}}$ (derivative of the sigmoid):

$$
\frac{\partial y_k}{\partial net_k^{(o)}} = f'(net_k^{(o)}) = y_k(1 - y_k)
$$

**(c)** $\dfrac{\partial net_k^{(o)}}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}}$ (from $net_k^{(o)} = \sum_j w_{jk}^{(2)} h_j$):

$$
\frac{\partial net_k^{(o)}}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}} = h_j
$$

Combining all three:

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}} = -(d_k - y_k) \cdot y_k(1 - y_k) \cdot h_j
$$

Substituting back into the gradient-descent update:

$$
\Delta w_{jk}^{(2)} = -\eta \left[-(d_k - y_k) y_k(1 - y_k) h_j\right] = \eta (d_k - y_k) y_k(1 - y_k) h_j
$$

**Define the output-layer error signal** $\delta_k^{(o)}$ as:

$$
\delta_k^{(o)} = (d_k - y_k) \, y_k(1 - y_k)
$$

Therefore, the **final update rule for output weights** is:

$$
\boxed{\,w_{jk}^{(2)}(t+1) = w_{jk}^{(2)}(t) + \eta \, \delta_k^{(o)} \, h_j\,}
$$

---

#### **Step 4: Derive the Update Rule for Hidden-Layer Weights $w_{ij}^{(1)}$**

Apply gradient descent again:

$$
\Delta w_{ij}^{(1)} = -\eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}}
$$

Chain rule — now $E$ depends on every $y_k$, each of which depends on $net_k^{(o)}$, which depends on $h_j$, which depends on $net_j^{(h)}$, which depends on $w_{ij}^{(1)}$:

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k}{\partial net_k^{(o)}} \cdot \frac{\partial net_k^{(o)}}{\partial h_j} \cdot \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial net_j^{(h)}} \cdot \frac{\partial net_j^{(h)}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}}
$$

Evaluate each factor:

**(a)** $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} = -(d_k - y_k)$

**(b)** $\dfrac{\partial y_k}{\partial net_k^{(o)}} = y_k(1 - y_k)$

**(c)** $\dfrac{\partial net_k^{(o)}}{\partial h_j} = w_{jk}^{(2)}$ (from $net_k^{(o)} = \sum_j w_{jk}^{(2)} h_j$)

**(d)** $\dfrac{\partial h_j}{\partial net_j^{(h)}} = h_j(1 - h_j)$ (sigmoid derivative)

**(e)** $\dfrac{\partial net_j^{(h)}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = x_i$

Combining:

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = -(d_k - y_k) \cdot y_k(1 - y_k) \cdot w_{jk}^{(2)} \cdot h_j(1 - h_j) \cdot x_i
$$

Summing over all output neurons $k$ and substituting the output delta $\delta_k^{(o)} = (d_k - y_k) y_k(1 - y_k)$:

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = -h_j(1 - h_j) \, x_i \sum_{k=1}^{m} \delta_k^{(o)} w_{jk}^{(2)}
$$

**Define the hidden-layer error signal** $\delta_j^{(h)}$ as:

$$
\delta_j^{(h)} = h_j(1 - h_j) \sum_{k=1}^{m} \delta_k^{(o)} w_{jk}^{(2)}
$$

Therefore, the **final update rule for hidden weights** is:

$$
\boxed{\,w_{ij}^{(1)}(t+1) = w_{ij}^{(1)}(t) + \eta \, \delta_j^{(h)} \, x_i\,}
$$

---

#### **Step 5: Summary of the Derived Back Propagation Learning Law**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forward pass:} \quad & net_j^{(h)} = \sum_i w_{ij}^{(1)} x_i, \quad h_j = f(net_j^{(h)}) \\
& net_k^{(o)} = \sum_j w_{jk}^{(2)} h_j, \quad y_k = f(net_k^{(o)}) \\[4pt]
\text{Error:} \quad & E = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_k (d_k - y_k)^2 \\[4pt]
\text{Backward pass:} \quad & \delta_k^{(o)} = (d_k - y_k) \, y_k(1 - y_k) \\
& \delta_j^{(h)} = h_j(1 - h_j) \sum_k \delta_k^{(o)} w_{jk}^{(2)} \\[4pt]
\text{Weight update:} \quad & w_{jk}^{(2)} \leftarrow w_{jk}^{(2)} + \eta \, \delta_k^{(o)} h_j \\
& w_{ij}^{(1)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(1)} + \eta \, \delta_j^{(h)} x_i
\end{aligned}
$$

This completes the rigorous derivation of the **Back Propagation Learning Law** (also called the **Generalized Delta Rule for multilayer networks**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The term $y_k(1-y_k)$ is the slope of the sigmoid at the operating point. When $y_k \to 0$ or $y_k \to 1$, the slope $\to 0$, which makes $\delta \to 0$ — this phenomenon is the **vanishing-gradient problem**, a key limitation of deep sigmoid networks and the motivation for ReLU.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

The following diagram depicts the **two-phase operational flow** of the Back Propagation Learning Law — the forward activation pass followed by the backward error-propagation pass.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize Weights Randomly w1 w2] --> B[Present Input Pattern x and Desired Output d]
    B --> C[Phase 1: FORWARD PASS]
    C --> C1[Compute net_h = sum w1 x at Hidden Layer]
    C1 --> C2[Apply Sigmoid: h = f net_h]
    C2 --> C3[Compute net_o = sum w2 h at Output Layer]
    C3 --> C4[Apply Sigmoid: y = f net_o]
    C4 --> D[Compute Error E = 0.5 times sum d minus y squared]
    D --> E{Error E less than threshold epsilon}
    E -- No --> F[Phase 2: BACKWARD PASS]
    F --> F1[Compute Output Delta delta_o = d minus y times y1 minus y]
    F1 --> F2[Propagate to Hidden: delta_h = h1 minus h times sum delta_o w2]
    F2 --> G1[Update Output Weights: w2 new = w2 old + eta delta_o h]
    G1 --> G2[Update Hidden Weights: w1 new = w1 old + eta delta_h x]
    G2 --> H[Increment Epoch t = t + 1]
    H --> B
    E -- Yes --> Z[Stop: Weights Converged - Network Trained]
```

### 4.2 Network Topology with Weight & Signal Labels

The next diagram shows a 2-2-1 MLP (2 inputs, 2 hidden, 1 output) annotated with the exact variables used in the derivation above.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1((x1)) -->|w11| H1((h1))
    X2((x2)) -->|w21| H1
    X1 -->|w12| H2((h2))
    X2 -->|w22| H2
    H1 -->|v11| Y((y))
    H2 -->|v21| Y
    Y --> E[E = 0.5 times d minus y squared]
    E --> D1[delta_o]
    D1 --> H1
    D1 --> H2
    H1 --> U1[Update w11 w21 w12 w22]
    H2 --> U1
    D1 --> U2[Update v11 v21]
    Y --> U2
```

### 4.3 Algorithmic Pseudocode (Mathematical Form, Not a Programming Language)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is presented as a **mathematical algorithm specification** — a sequence of formal mathematical steps — *not* a programming-language implementation, in compliance with KTU non-programming-subject conventions for theoretical derivations.

**Algorithm: Back Propagation Learning Law**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Input: Training set } \{(\mathbf{x}^{(p)}, \mathbf{d}^{(p)})\}_{p=1}^{P}, \text{ learning rate } \eta, \text{ max epochs } T, \text{ tolerance } \epsilon \\
& \text{Output: Trained weights } \mathbf{W}^{(1)}, \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \\
& \textbf{Initialize: } \mathbf{W}^{(1)}, \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \sim \mathcal{U}(-\varepsilon, \varepsilon) \\
& \text{For } t = 1, 2, \ldots, T: \\
& \quad \text{Shuffle training set} \\
& \quad \text{For each pattern } p = 1, 2, \ldots, P: \\
& \quad\quad \text{1. Forward pass: compute } \mathbf{h}^{(p)}, \mathbf{y}^{(p)} \\
& \quad\quad \text{2. Compute } E^{(p)} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_k (d_k^{(p)} - y_k^{(p)})^2 \\
& \quad\quad \text{3. Compute } \delta_k^{(o)} = (d_k - y_k) y_k (1 - y_k) \\
& \quad\quad \text{4. Compute } \delta_j^{(h)} = h_j (1 - h_j) \sum_k \delta_k^{(o)} w_{jk}^{(2)} \\
& \quad\quad \text{5. Update: } w_{jk}^{(2)} \leftarrow w_{jk}^{(2)} + \eta \, \delta_k^{(o)} h_j \\
& \quad\quad \text{6. Update: } w_{ij}^{(1)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(1)} + \eta \, \delta_j^{(h)} x_i \\
& \quad \text{If } \tfrac{1}{P}\sum_p E^{(p)} < \epsilon: \text{ return } \mathbf{W}^{(1)}, \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \\
& \text{return } \mathbf{W}^{(1)}, \mathbf{W}^{(2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – April 2024, Model Paper Style]`**

**Q1. State the Back Propagation Learning Law and write the generalized delta rule for updating the output and hidden layer weights.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Back Propagation Learning Law is a supervised, gradient-based weight-update procedure for multilayer feed-forward networks. It propagates the output error backward through the network and updates each weight in the direction that minimizes the error.

$$
w_{ij}^{new} = w_{ij}^{old} + \Delta w_{ij}, \quad \Delta w_{ij} = -\eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}}
$$

The generalized delta rule is:

$$
w_{jk}^{(2)} \leftarrow w_{jk}^{(2)} + \eta \, (d_k - y_k) y_k(1-y_k) \, h_j \quad \text{(output layer)}
$$

$$
w_{ij}^{(1)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(1)} + \eta \, \left[h_j(1-h_j) \sum_k (d_k-y_k)y_k(1-y_k) w_{jk}^{(2)}\right] x_i \quad \text{(hidden layer)}
$$

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`**

**Q2. Why is the sigmoid activation function preferred in the classical back propagation algorithm? What is its main limitation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The sigmoid $f(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ is preferred because its derivative has a simple, self-expressive form: $f'(x) = f(x)[1 - f(x)] = y(1-y)$. This greatly simplifies the computation of the local gradient $\delta$ during the backward pass, since the activation output $y$ is already available from the forward pass — no extra derivative calculation is needed.

**Main limitation:** The vanishing-gradient problem — when $|x|$ is large, $f'(x) \to 0$, causing the deltas in deep layers to become negligibly small and effectively halting learning in early layers.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – April 2024 Style, Module 2]`**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the back propagation learning law for updating the weights between the hidden layer and the output layer of a multilayer feed-forward neural network. Clearly state the chain-rule steps and define the output error signal $\delta_k^{(o)}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the update rule for the weights between the input and hidden layer. Show that the hidden-layer error signal depends on the sum of the weighted output deltas. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Setup.** Consider a network with input $\mathbf{x}$, hidden output $h_j = f(\sum_i w_{ij}^{(1)} x_i)$, and final output $y_k = f(\sum_j w_{jk}^{(2)} h_j)$. The error is:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{m}(d_k - y_k)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Gradient descent on $w_{jk}^{(2)}$.** The weight change is:

$$
\Delta w_{jk}^{(2)} = -\eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the chain rule:**

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k}{\partial net_k^{(o)}} \cdot \frac{\partial net_k^{(o)}}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}} = -(d_k - y_k) \cdot y_k(1-y_k) \cdot h_j
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$
\Delta w_{jk}^{(2)} = \eta (d_k - y_k) y_k(1-y_k) h_j
$$

**Step 5 — Define the output error signal:**

$$
\delta_k^{(o)} = (d_k - y_k) y_k(1-y_k)
$$

**Step 6 — Final update rule:**

$$
\boxed{\,w_{jk}^{(2)}(t+1) = w_{jk}^{(2)}(t) + \eta \, \delta_k^{(o)} \, h_j\,}
$$

*(Full marks awarded for stating the loss function, applying the chain rule in three factors, defining $\delta_k^{(o)}$, and writing the final update.)*

---

**(b)** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Apply gradient descent:**

$$
\Delta w_{ij}^{(1)} = -\eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}}
$$

**Step 2 — Chain rule (longer chain, since $E$ reaches $w_{ij}^{(1)}$ through every $y_k$ and every $net_k^{(o)}$):**

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = \sum_{k=1}^{m}\frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k}\cdot\frac{\partial y_k}{\partial net_k^{(o)}}\cdot\frac{\partial net_k^{(o)}}{\partial h_j}\cdot\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial net_j^{(h)}}\cdot\frac{\partial net_j^{(h)}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} &= -(d_k - y_k) \\
\frac{\partial y_k}{\partial net_k^{(o)}} &= y_k(1-y_k) \\
\frac{\partial net_k^{(o)}}{\partial h_j} &= w_{jk}^{(2)} \\
\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial net_j^{(h)}} &= h_j(1-h_j) \\
\frac{\partial net_j^{(h)}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} &= x_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply and sum over $k$:**

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = -h_j(1-h_j) \, x_i \sum_{k=1}^{m}(d_k - y_k) y_k(1-y_k) w_{jk}^{(2)}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the hidden error signal:**

$$
\delta_j^{(h)} = h_j(1-h_j) \sum_{k=1}^{m}\delta_k^{(o)} w_{jk}^{(2)}
$$

**Step 6 — Final update rule:**

$$
\boxed{\,w_{ij}^{(1)}(t+1) = w_{ij}^{(1)}(t) + \eta \, \delta_j^{(h)} \, x_i\,}
$$

**Key insight:** $\delta_j^{(h)}$ is computed by taking the output deltas, scaling them by the connecting weights, summing them, and then multiplying by the hidden neuron's sigmoid derivative — the classic *back propagation* step.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the two phases (forward pass and backward pass) of the back propagation algorithm with a neat flowchart and state the local gradient computation at each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-input, 2-hidden, 1-output network uses sigmoid activation. The input is $\mathbf{x} = (0.5, 0.8)$, the desired output is $d = 0.4$, and the weights are:

$$
\mathbf{W}^{(1)} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.3 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{W}^{(2)} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Take $\eta = 0.5$. Perform **one full forward pass** and **one weight-update step** (backward pass). Show all deltas and weight changes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *(7 marks — described conceptually)*

- **Forward Pass:** Inputs propagate from left to right; each neuron computes $net = \sum w x$ and applies the sigmoid $y = f(net)$. No weight changes occur.
- **Backward Pass:** The error $E$ is computed at the output; local gradients $\delta_k^{(o)}$ and $\delta_j^{(h)}$ are computed and propagated right-to-left; weights are updated as $w \leftarrow w + \eta \delta \cdot (\text{input})$.
- **Local Gradients:** At output $\delta_k^{(o)} = (d_k - y_k) y_k(1-y_k)$; at hidden $\delta_j^{(h)} = h_j(1-h_j) \sum_k \delta_k^{(o)} w_{jk}^{(2)}$.

*(The flowchart given in SECTION 4.1 above is to be reproduced; full marks for the two-phase explanation, the local-gradient formulas, and the flowchart.)*

---

**(b)** *(7 marks — numerical)*

**Forward pass:**

Hidden nets:

$$
net_1^{(h)} = 0.3(0.5) + 0.2(0.8) = 0.15 + 0.16 = 0.31
$$

$$
net_2^{(h)} = 0.1(0.5) + 0.4(0.8) = 0.05 + 0.32 = 0.37
$$

Hidden outputs (sigmoid $f(x)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$):

$$
h_1 = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.31}} = \frac{1}{1.733} \approx 0.5771
$$

$$
h_2 = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.37}} = \frac{1}{1.691} \approx 0.5914
$$

Output net:

$$
net^{(o)} = 0.5(0.5771) + 0.6(0.5914) = 0.2886 + 0.3548 = 0.6434
$$

Output:

$$
y = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.6434}} = \frac{1}{1.8025} \approx 0.5548
$$

**Backward pass:**

Error gradient term:

$$
(d - y) = 0.4 - 0.5548 = -0.1548
$$

Output delta:

$$
\delta^{(o)} = (d-y) \cdot y(1-y) = (-0.1548)(0.5548)(0.4452) \approx -0.0382
$$

Hidden deltas:

$$
\delta_1^{(h)} = h_1(1-h_1) \cdot \delta^{(o)} \cdot w_{11}^{(2)} = (0.5771)(0.4229)(-0.0382)(0.5) \approx -0.00466
$$

$$
\delta_2^{(h)} = h_2(1-h_2) \cdot \delta^{(o)} \cdot w_{21}^{(2)} = (0.5914)(0.4086)(-0.0382)(0.6) \approx -0.00553
$$

**Weight updates (with $\eta = 0.5$):**

Output weights:

$$
w_{11}^{(2)} \leftarrow 0.5 + (0.5)(-0.0382)(0.5771) \approx 0.5 - 0.01102 \approx 0.48898
$$

$$
w_{21}^{(2)} \leftarrow 0.6 + (0.5)(-0.0382)(0.5914) \approx 0.6 - 0.01130 \approx 0.58870
$$

Hidden weights (from $x_1 = 0.5, x_2 = 0.8$):

$$
w_{11}^{(1)} \leftarrow 0.3 + (0.5)(-0.00466)(0.5) \approx 0.3 - 0.00117 \approx 0.29883
$$

$$
w_{21}^{(1)} \leftarrow 0.1 + (0.5)(-0.00466)(0.8) \approx 0.1 - 0.00186 \approx 0.09814
$$

$$
w_{12}^{(1)} \leftarrow 0.2 + (0.5)(-0.00553)(0.5) \approx 0.2 - 0.00138 \approx 0.19862
$$

$$
w_{22}^{(1)} \leftarrow 0.4 + (0.5)(-0.00553)(0.8) \approx 0.4 - 0.00221 \approx 0.39779
$$

These are the updated weights after one epoch of back propagation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in gradient descent** — students often write $\Delta w = +\eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w}$ (without the minus sign). KTU examiners specifically deduct ½–1 mark for this.
> 2. **Missing the $\frac{1}{2}$ in $E$** — without the $\frac{1}{2}$, the chain rule yields $2(d_k - y_k)$ instead of $(d_k - y_k)$. The error signal $\delta$ becomes wrong, and the entire numerical answer is incorrect.
> 3. **Confusing the order of $w_{ij}$ indices** — KTU follows the convention "$w_{ij}$ = weight *from* $i$ *to* $j$". Reversing this causes cascading sign errors.
> 4. **Not showing all three chain-rule factors explicitly** — examiners expect each partial derivative written out as a separate step ($\partial E / \partial y$, $\partial y / \partial net$, $\partial net / \partial w$). Skipping any factor costs 1 mark each.
> 5. **Stopping at "$\delta$" without defining it** — always give the explicit formula for $\delta_k^{(o)}$ and $\delta_j^{(h)}$ in your final answer.
> 6. **Numerical problems:** students often forget to recompute the *sigmoid* for the *new* $h$ values in the next epoch, or they use rounded intermediate values without stating so — KTU allows rounding to 4 decimals, but the rounding must be consistent.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Back Propagation Learning Law**

- **Core idea:** Use *gradient descent* on the squared error $E = \frac{1}{2}\sum(d_k - y_k)^2$, with the *chain rule* to find $\partial E / \partial w$ for every weight.
- **Two phases:** **Forward pass** (compute $net$, $y$) → **Backward pass** (compute $\delta$, update $w$).
- **Output delta:** $\delta_k^{(o)} = (d_k - y_k) \, y_k(1-y_k)$.
- **Hidden delta:** $\delta_j^{(h)} = h_j(1-h_j) \sum_k \delta_k^{(o)} w_{jk}^{(2)}$ — this is the *back-propagation* step that gives the algorithm its name.
- **Weight update (output):** $w_{jk}^{(2)} \leftarrow w_{jk}^{(2)} + \eta \, \delta_k^{(o)} h_j$.
- **Weight update (hidden):** $w_{ij}^{(1)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(1)} + \eta \, \delta_j^{(h)} x_i$.
- **Sigmoid derivative trick:** $f'(x) = f(x)[1 - f(x)]$ — this is the *only* reason the sigmoid is used in classical BP.
- **Learning rate** $\eta$ must be small (typically $0.1$–$0.5$) — too large ⇒ oscillation; too small ⇒ slow convergence.
- **Local vs. global minimum:** BP only guarantees convergence to a *local* minimum, not the *global* minimum.
- **Stopping criteria:** $E < \epsilon$ (pre-set tolerance), OR maximum number of epochs reached, OR validation error begins to increase (early stopping).
- **Common enhancements:** *Momentum* ($\alpha \Delta w(t)$ added), *adaptive learning rates*, *batch vs. stochastic vs. mini-batch* updates.
- **Vanishing gradient:** classical BP suffers from this in deep networks — motivation for ReLU, LSTM, and residual connections.
- **Initialization:** weights must be *small and random* (not zero) to break symmetry.
- **KTU keywords to memorize:** *Generalized Delta Rule, Gradient Descent, Chain Rule, Sigmoid Derivative, Local Gradient, Forward Pass, Backward Pass, Vanishing Gradient, Learning Rate, Epoch.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Back Propagation Learning Algorithm (Derivation of the Algorithm is Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT403 - MACHINE LEARNING II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Back Propagation Learning Algorithm (Derivation of the Algorithm is Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Back Propagation (BP)** is a **supervised learning algorithm** used to train **Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs)** / **Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs)**. It efficiently computes the **gradient of the loss function** with respect to the network's weights by applying the **chain rule of calculus** in a *layer-wise reverse manner*, propagating the error signal from the output layer back to the input layer.

Mathematically, given a network with loss function $C$ and weights $w_{jk}^{\ell}$ (weight from neuron $k$ in layer $\ell-1$ to neuron $j$ in layer $\ell$), Back Propagation computes $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial w_{jk}^{\ell}}$ and $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial b_j^{\ell}}$ for **every** weight and bias in the network in **two passes**: a *forward pass* (to compute activations and the loss) and a *backward pass* (to propagate error gradients using the chain rule).

### Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Glossary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Weight | $w_{jk}^{\ell}$ | Synaptic strength between neuron $k$ (layer $\ell-1$) and neuron $j$ (layer $\ell$) |
| Bias | $b_j^{\ell}$ | Threshold offset of neuron $j$ in layer $\ell$ |
| Weighted Input | $z_j^{\ell}$ | Pre-activation: $\sum_k w_{jk}^{\ell} a_k^{\ell-1} + b_j^{\ell}$ |
| Activation | $a_j^{\ell}$ | Post-activation: $\sigma(z_j^{\ell})$ |
| Loss / Cost | $C$ | Scalar measure of network error (e.g., MSE, Cross-Entropy) |
| Error Term | $\delta_j^{\ell}$ | Local gradient at neuron $j$ in layer $\ell$ |
| Learning Rate | $\eta$ | Step-size hyper-parameter for gradient descent |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🎯

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Student Taking an Exam and Reviewing Mistakes**

Imagine a student solving a multi-step mathematics problem in an exam:

1. **Forward Pass (Solving the Problem):** The student works from the *first step* to the *last step*, computing intermediate answers. Each step builds on the previous one — analogous to a neural network propagating activations layer by layer.

2. **Loss Computation (Checking the Final Answer):** At the end, the student compares their final answer with the correct answer and calculates *how wrong* they were. This is the **loss** $C$.

3. **Backward Pass (Reviewing Mistakes):** The student now reflects **backwards**: "If my final answer is off by $0.5$, how much did Step 3 contribute to that error? How much did Step 2? How much did Step 1?" This reverse attribution of blame is **exactly what backpropagation does** with the chain rule.

4. **Weight Update (Correcting Mistakes):** The student adjusts the steps that contributed the most to the error — analogous to updating weights via **gradient descent**: $w \leftarrow w - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial w}$.

**Key Insight:** Just as a student would never *randomly* change their methodology, backpropagation *precisely* identifies **which weights** in **which layers** are most responsible for the error, and adjusts them by an amount proportional to their contribution.

## 1.3 Why Backpropagation is Revolutionary

> [!TIP]
> Before backpropagation (pre-1986), training multi-layer networks was computationally intractable because the gradient had to be estimated numerically for each weight — a cost of $O(W^2)$ where $W$ is the number of weights. Backpropagation reduces this to $O(W)$ by exploiting the chain rule and the layered structure of the network. This is the **single most important algorithm in deep learning history**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline — The Four Equations of Backpropagation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to memorize and apply the **four fundamental equations** of backpropagation. These equations are the **backbone** of any derivation question.

### Equation BP1: Output Layer Error Term

$$\delta_j^{L} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial a_j^{L}} \cdot \sigma^{\prime}(z_j^{L})$$

**Meaning:** The error at an output neuron is the product of (a) how fast the cost changes with the output activation, and (b) how fast the activation changes with the weighted input.

---

### Equation BP2: Hidden Layer Error Term (The Core of Backpropagation)

$$\delta_j^{\ell} = \sum_k w_{kj}^{\ell+1} \, \delta_k^{\ell+1} \cdot \sigma^{\prime}(z_j^{\ell})$$

In **matrix form**:

$$\delta^{\ell} = \left( (W^{\ell+1})^{T} \delta^{\ell+1} \right) \odot \sigma^{\prime}(z^{\ell})$$

**Meaning:** The error at a hidden neuron is computed by taking a *weighted sum* of the errors from the next layer, multiplied by the derivative of the activation function. The symbol $\odot$ denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product**.

---

### Equation BP3: Rate of Change of Cost with respect to Bias

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial b_j^{\ell}} = \delta_j^{\ell}$$

**Meaning:** The gradient with respect to a bias is **exactly equal to the error term** of that neuron.

---

### Equation BP4: Rate of Change of Cost with respect to Weight

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial w_{jk}^{\ell}} = a_k^{\ell-1} \, \delta_j^{\ell}$$

In **matrix form**:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial W^{\ell}} = \delta^{\ell} \, (a^{\ell-1})^{T}$$

**Meaning:** The gradient with respect to a weight is the product of the **error term** at the output end and the **activation** at the input end.

## 2.2 Activation Functions & Their Derivatives (Critical for Derivations)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **derivative** of activation functions because they appear inside the backpropagation equations. Memorize these!

| Activation | Formula $\sigma(z)$ | Derivative $\sigma'(z)$ | Output Range |
|------------|---------------------|--------------------------|----------------|
| **Sigmoid (Logistic)** | $\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | $(0,1)$ |
| **Tanh (Hyperbolic Tangent)** | $\tanh(z)$ | $1-\tanh^{2}(z)$ | $(-1,1)$ |
| **ReLU** | $\max(0,z)$ | $\begin{cases} 1 & z > 0 \\ 0 & z \le 0 \end{cases}$ | $[0,\infty)$ |
| **Leaky ReLU** | $\max(\alpha z, z)$ | $\begin{cases} 1 & z > 0 \\ \alpha & z \le 0 \end{cases}$ | $(-\infty,\infty)$ |
| **Softmax** | $\dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | $\sigma_i(\delta_{ij}-\sigma_j)$ | $(0,1)$, sum = 1 |

## 2.3 Loss Functions Used in Derivations

> [!TIP]
> For backpropagation problems, KTU commonly uses **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** or **Cross-Entropy Loss**. Knowing $\frac{\partial C}{\partial a_j^L}$ for each is essential.

| Loss Function | Formula $C$ | Derivative $\frac{\partial C}{\partial a_j^{L}}$ |
|----------------|---------------|------------------------------------------------|
| **MSE (Half)** | $\dfrac{1}{2}\sum_j (a_j^{L}-y_j)^2$ | $(a_j^{L}-y_j)$ |
| **Cross-Entropy** | $-\sum_j y_j \ln(a_j^{L})$ | $-\dfrac{y_j}{a_j^{L}}$ |
| **Quadratic Cost** | $\dfrac{1}{2n}\sum_x \sum_j (a_j^{L}(x)-y_j(x))^2$ | $\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_x (a_j^{L}(x)-y_j(x))$ |

## 2.4 Weight Update Rule (Gradient Descent Step)

After computing the gradients, weights are updated as:

$$w_{jk}^{\ell} \leftarrow w_{jk}^{\ell} - \eta \frac{\partial C}{\partial w_{jk}^{\ell}}$$

$$b_j^{\ell} \leftarrow b_j^{\ell} - \eta \frac{\partial C}{\partial b_j^{\ell}}$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (typically $0.001$ to $0.1$).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table** — these are the equations that solve 90% of KTU backpropagation questions.

| # | Equation | Form | Purpose |
|---|----------|------|---------|
| 1 | $z_j^{\ell} = \sum_k w_{jk}^{\ell} a_k^{\ell-1} + b_j^{\ell}$ | Scalar | Pre-activation |
| 2 | $a_j^{\ell} = \sigma(z_j^{\ell})$ | Scalar | Activation |
| 3 | $z^{\ell} = W^{\ell} a^{\ell-1} + b^{\ell}$ | Vector | Pre-activation (vectorized) |
| 4 | $a^{\ell} = \sigma(z^{\ell})$ | Vector | Activation (vectorized) |
| 5 | $\delta_j^{L} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial a_j^{L}} \sigma^{\prime}(z_j^{L})$ | Scalar | Output error |
| 6 | $\delta^{\ell} = ((W^{\ell+1})^{T} \delta^{\ell+1}) \odot \sigma^{\prime}(z^{\ell})$ | Vector | Hidden error |
| 7 | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial b_j^{\ell}} = \delta_j^{\ell}$ | Scalar | Bias gradient |
| 8 | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial w_{jk}^{\ell}} = a_k^{\ell-1} \delta_j^{\ell}$ | Scalar | Weight gradient |
| 9 | $w \leftarrow w - \eta \nabla_w C$ | Vector | Update rule |
| 10 | $C_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_j (a_j^{L}-y_j)^2$ | Scalar | Loss (MSE) |

## 2.6 Engineering & Real-World Utility

Backpropagation is the engine behind virtually every modern AI system:
- **Computer Vision:** CNNs trained via backpropagation power facial recognition, medical imaging diagnostics, and autonomous vehicle perception.
- **Natural Language Processing:** RNNs, LSTMs, and Transformers rely on a backpropagation variant called **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**.
- **Speech Recognition:** Deep acoustic models (used in Alexa, Siri, Google Assistant) are trained using backpropagation.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Policy gradient methods use backpropagation to train neural policies for game-playing agents (AlphaGo, AlphaZero).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme **explicitly requires** the derivation of the backpropagation algorithm. Below is the **exhaustive, line-by-line derivation** with no steps skipped.

## 3.1 Notation & Setup

Consider a feedforward neural network with $L$ layers. Layer $\ell$ has neurons indexed $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n_\ell$. We define:

$$z_j^{\ell} = \sum_k w_{jk}^{\ell} a_k^{\ell-1} + b_j^{\ell}$$

$$a_j^{\ell} = \sigma(z_j^{\ell})$$

with the convention that $a^0 = x$ (the input vector). The cost function is the average over training examples:

$$C = \frac{1}{n} \sum_x C_x$$

Since the gradient $\frac{\partial C}{\partial w} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_x \frac{\partial C_x}{\partial w}$, we can derive everything for a single training example and average at the end.

## 3.2 DERIVATION 1: The Output Error Term $\delta_j^{L}$ (BP1)

We define the error term:

$$\delta_j^{\ell} \equiv \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_j^{\ell}}$$

**Why this definition?** Because $z_j^{\ell}$ is the immediate "lever" we can pull to change the network's output. Small changes to $z_j^{\ell}$ propagate to small changes in $C$ at a rate given by $\delta_j^{\ell}$.

For the **output layer** $\ell = L$, applying the **chain rule**:

$$\delta_j^{L} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_j^{L}} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial a_j^{L}} \cdot \frac{\partial a_j^{L}}{\partial z_j^{L}}$$

Since $a_j^{L} = \sigma(z_j^{L})$, we have $\frac{\partial a_j^{L}}{\partial z_j^{L}} = \sigma^{\prime}(z_j^{L})$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\delta_j^{L} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial a_j^{L}} \cdot \sigma^{\prime}(z_j^{L})} \quad \text{(BP1, derived)}$$

**Concrete Example (MSE loss):** If $C = \frac{1}{2}\sum_j (a_j^L - y_j)^2$, then $\frac{\partial C}{\partial a_j^L} = (a_j^L - y_j)$, so:

$$\delta_j^{L} = (a_j^{L} - y_j) \cdot \sigma^{\prime}(z_j^{L})$$

In matrix form: $\delta^{L} = \nabla_a C \odot \sigma^{\prime}(z^{L})$.

## 3.3 DERIVATION 2: The Hidden Layer Error Term $\delta_j^{\ell}$ (BP2)

For a **hidden layer** $\ell < L$, we need to express $\delta_j^{\ell} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_j^{\ell}}$ in terms of errors in the *next* layer $\ell+1$.

**Step 1:** Apply the chain rule, expanding through the weighted inputs of layer $\ell+1$:

$$\delta_j^{\ell} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_j^{\ell}} = \sum_k \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_k^{\ell+1}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_k^{\ell+1}}{\partial z_j^{\ell}}$$

**Step 2:** Recognize the first factor:

$$\sum_k \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_k^{\ell+1}} = \sum_k \delta_k^{\ell+1}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the second factor. From the definition of $z_k^{\ell+1}$:

$$z_k^{\ell+1} = \sum_j w_{kj}^{\ell+1} a_j^{\ell} + b_k^{\ell+1} = \sum_j w_{kj}^{\ell+1} \sigma(z_j^{\ell}) + b_k^{\ell+1}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial z_k^{\ell+1}}{\partial z_j^{\ell}} = w_{kj}^{\ell+1} \cdot \sigma^{\prime}(z_j^{\ell})$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$\delta_j^{\ell} = \sum_k \delta_k^{\ell+1} \cdot w_{kj}^{\ell+1} \cdot \sigma^{\prime}(z_j^{\ell})$$

**Step 5:** Reorder (the $\sigma^{\prime}$ term doesn't depend on $k$):

$$\delta_j^{\ell} = \sigma^{\prime}(z_j^{\ell}) \sum_k w_{kj}^{\ell+1} \delta_k^{\ell+1}$$

**Step 6 (Vector/Matrix form):** Writing $\delta^{\ell}, \delta^{\ell+1}$ as column vectors, the sum becomes $(W^{\ell+1})^{T} \delta^{\ell+1}$, and we use the Hadamard product $\odot$:

$$\boxed{\delta^{\ell} = \left( (W^{\ell+1})^{T} \delta^{\ell+1} \right) \odot \sigma^{\prime}(z^{\ell})} \quad \text{(BP2, derived)}$$

## 3.4 DERIVATION 3: Gradient with respect to Bias (BP3)

We want $\frac{\partial C}{\partial b_j^{\ell}}$. From the definition of $z_j^{\ell}$:

$$z_j^{\ell} = \sum_k w_{jk}^{\ell} a_k^{\ell-1} + b_j^{\ell}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial z_j^{\ell}}{\partial b_j^{\ell}} = 1$$

Applying the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial b_j^{\ell}} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_j^{\ell}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_j^{\ell}}{\partial b_j^{\ell}} = \delta_j^{\ell} \cdot 1$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C}{\partial b_j^{\ell}} = \delta_j^{\ell}} \quad \text{(BP3, derived)}$$

## 3.5 DERIVATION 4: Gradient with respect to Weight (BP4)

We want $\frac{\partial C}{\partial w_{jk}^{\ell}}$. From the definition:

$$z_j^{\ell} = \sum_k w_{jk}^{\ell} a_k^{\ell-1} + b_j^{\ell}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial z_j^{\ell}}{\partial w_{jk}^{\ell}} = a_k^{\ell-1}$$

Applying the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial w_{jk}^{\ell}} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_j^{\ell}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_j^{\ell}}{\partial w_{jk}^{\ell}} = \delta_j^{\ell} \cdot a_k^{\ell-1}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C}{\partial w_{jk}^{\ell}} = a_k^{\ell-1} \delta_j^{\ell}} \quad \text{(BP4, derived)}$$

In matrix form:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial W^{\ell}} = \delta^{\ell} (a^{\ell-1})^{T}$$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!TIP]
> This is a typical KTU 14-mark problem. Work through it carefully.

**Network:** 2 inputs $\to$ 2 hidden neurons (sigmoid) $\to$ 1 output (sigmoid). Use **MSE loss**. Given one training example:

$$x = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad y = 1$$

**Initial weights (Layer 1: $W^1$, biases $b^1$):**

$$W^{1} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix}, \quad b^{1} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Initial weights (Layer 2: $W^2$, bias $b^2$):**

$$W^{2} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}, \quad b^{2} = 0.1$$

**Learning rate:** $\eta = 0.5$. Compute **one** complete forward + backward pass.

### Step A — Forward Pass

**Layer 1 pre-activation:**

$$z^1 = W^1 a^0 + b^1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 + 0.1 \\ 0.3 + 0.2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Layer 1 activation (sigmoid):**

$$a^1 = \sigma(z^1) = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma(0.2) \\ \sigma(0.5) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5498 \\ 0.6225 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Layer 2 pre-activation:**

$$z^2 = W^2 a^1 + b^2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.5498 \\ 0.6225 \end{pmatrix} + 0.1$$

$$z^2 = (0.5)(0.5498) + (0.6)(0.6225) + 0.1 = 0.2749 + 0.3735 + 0.1 = 0.7484$$

**Layer 2 activation (output):**

$$a^{2} = \sigma(z^2) = \sigma(0.7484) = 0.6787$$

**Loss (MSE):**

$$C = \frac{1}{2}(a^2 - y)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.6787 - 1)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.1033) = 0.05165$$

### Step B — Backward Pass

**Compute $\delta^2$ (output error, BP1):**

Using $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$:

$$\sigma'(z^2) = 0.6787 \times (1 - 0.6787) = 0.6787 \times 0.3213 = 0.2181$$

$$\delta^2 = (a^2 - y) \cdot \sigma'(z^2) = (0.6787 - 1) \times 0.2181 = (-0.3213)(0.2181) = -0.0701$$

**Compute $\delta^1$ (hidden error, BP2):**

$$\sigma'(z^1) = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5498(1-0.5498) \\ 0.6225(1-0.6225) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2475 \\ 0.2350 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\delta^1 = (W^2)^T \delta^2 \odot \sigma'(z^1)$$

$$(W^2)^T \delta^2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.6 \end{pmatrix} (-0.0701) = \begin{pmatrix} -0.0350 \\ -0.0420 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\delta^1 = \begin{pmatrix} -0.0350 \\ -0.0420 \end{pmatrix} \odot \begin{pmatrix} 0.2475 \\ 0.2350 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -0.00867 \\ -0.00987 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Compute gradients (BP3 & BP4):**

**Bias gradients:**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial b^2} = \delta^2 = -0.0701, \quad \frac{\partial C}{\partial b^1} = \delta^1 = \begin{pmatrix} -0.00867 \\ -0.00987 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Weight gradients (matrix form):**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial W^2} = \delta^2 (a^1)^T = (-0.0701) \begin{pmatrix} 0.5498 & 0.6225 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -0.0385 & -0.0436 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial W^1} = \delta^1 (a^0)^T = \begin{pmatrix} -0.00867 \\ -0.00987 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -0.00867 & 0 \\ -0.00987 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step C — Weight Update

$$W^2_{\text{new}} = W^2 - \eta \frac{\partial C}{\partial W^2} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix} - 0.5 \begin{pmatrix} -0.0385 & -0.0436 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$W^2_{\text{new}} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 + 0.01925 & 0.6 + 0.0218 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5193 & 0.6218 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$b^2_{\text{new}} = 0.1 - 0.5(-0.0701) = 0.1 + 0.0350 = 0.1350$$

$$W^1_{\text{new}} = W^1 - 0.5 \frac{\partial C}{\partial W^1} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 + 0.00434 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 + 0.00494 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1043 & 0.2 \\ 0.3049 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$b^1_{\text{new}} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 + 0.00434 \\ 0.2 + 0.00494 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1043 \\ 0.2049 \end{pmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Notice how the weight updates in the **first row of $W^1$** for input neuron 2 are **zero** because the input $a_2^0 = 0$. This demonstrates a key property: if an input activation is zero, the corresponding weight gradient vanishes, and the weight is **not updated**. This is the **"dying ReLU"** phenomenon generalized.

## 3.7 Summary of Derivation Chain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The complete logical chain of the backpropagation derivation:

**Step 1:** Define the error term $\delta_j^{\ell} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_j^{\ell}}$ (this is a *convenient* intermediate quantity).

**Step 2 (BP1):** Use the chain rule to relate $\delta^L$ to the cost derivative: $\delta^L = \frac{\partial C}{\partial a^L} \odot \sigma'(z^L)$.

**Step 3 (BP2):** Use the chain rule to express $\delta^{\ell}$ in terms of $\delta^{\ell+1}$: $\delta^{\ell} = ((W^{\ell+1})^T \delta^{\ell+1}) \odot \sigma'(z^{\ell})$.

**Step 4 (BP3):** Differentiate $z_j^{\ell}$ with respect to $b_j^{\ell}$: $\frac{\partial z_j^{\ell}}{\partial b_j^{\ell}} = 1$, giving $\frac{\partial C}{\partial b_j^{\ell}} = \delta_j^{\ell}$.

**Step 5 (BP4):** Differentiate $z_j^{\ell}$ with respect to $w_{jk}^{\ell}$: $\frac{\partial z_j^{\ell}}{\partial w_{jk}^{\ell}} = a_k^{\ell-1}$, giving $\frac{\partial C}{\partial w_{jk}^{\ell}} = a_k^{\ell-1} \delta_j^{\ell}$.

**Step 6:** Update all weights and biases simultaneously using $w \leftarrow w - \eta \nabla_w C$.

## 3.8 Algorithmic Pseudocode (For Reference)

```
ALGORITHM: Stochastic Gradient Descent with Backpropagation
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INPUT: Training set {(x⁽¹⁾, y⁽¹⁾), ..., (x⁽ⁿ⁾, y⁽ⁿ⁾)}}
       Learning rate η, Number of epochs M
       Network architecture with L layers

FOR epoch = 1 TO M:
    Shuffle training set
    
    FOR each training example (x, y):
        # ──── FORWARD PASS ────
        a⁰ ← x
        FOR ℓ = 1 TO L:
            z^ℓ ← W^ℓ · a^(ℓ-1) + b^ℓ
            a^ℓ ← σ(z^ℓ)
        END
        
        # ──── BACKWARD PASS ────
        # Compute output error
        δ^L ← ∇_a C ⊙ σ'(z^L)
        
        # Propagate error backwards
        FOR ℓ = L-1 DOWNTO 1:
            δ^ℓ ← ((W^(ℓ+1))^T · δ^(ℓ+1)) ⊙ σ'(z^ℓ)
        END
        
        # ──── GRADIENT UPDATE ────
        FOR ℓ = 1 TO L:
            ∂C/∂W^ℓ ← δ^ℓ · (a^(ℓ-1))^T
            ∂C/∂b^ℓ ← δ^ℓ
            W^ℓ ← W^ℓ - η · ∂C/∂W^ℓ
            b^ℓ ← b^ℓ - η · ∂C/∂b^ℓ
        END
    END
END
```

    loss -.->|delta L equals minus 0.07| onode1
    loss -.->|delta L equals minus 0.05| onode2

## 4.2 Forward Pass Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputBlock[Input Layer a0] -->|Matrix multiply plus bias| preAct1[Pre-activation z1]
    preAct1 -->|Apply sigma| act1[Activation a1]
    act1 -->|Matrix multiply plus bias| preAct2[Pre-activation z2]
    preAct2 -->|Apply sigma| act2[Activation a2 equals Output]
    act2 -->|Compare with y| costBlock[Cost C]
```

## 4.3 Backward Pass Block Diagram (Error Propagation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    costBlock[Cost C] -->|BP1: partial C by partial aL| gradOut[Gradient at output]
    gradOut -->|Multiply by sigma prime zL| deltaL[delta L: error at output]
    deltaL -->|BP2: Transpose W2 times delta L| deltaLMid[Intermediate vector]
    deltaLMid -->|Hadamard with sigma prime z1| delta1[delta 1: error at hidden]
    delta1 -->|BP3: equals delta| gradB[Gradient wrt bias]
    delta1 -->|BP4: outer product with a0| gradW[Gradient wrt weight]
    gradW -->|Subtract eta times gradW| updatedW[Updated weights]
    gradB -->|Subtract eta times gradB| updatedB[Updated biases]
```

## 4.4 Computational Graph of Backpropagation (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[x: input] --> node2[z1 equals W1 x plus b1]
    node2 --> node3[a1 equals sigma z1]
    node3 --> node4[z2 equals W2 a1 plus b2]
    node4 --> node5[a2 equals sigma z2]
    node5 --> node6[C equals half a2 minus y squared]
    node6 -.->|Backprop: partial C by partial a2| node5
    node5 -.->|Backprop: times sigma prime| node4
    node4 -.->|Backprop: times W2 transpose| node3
    node3 -.->|Backprop: times sigma prime| node2
    node2 -.->|Backprop: gradients w1 and b1| node1
```

## 4.5 Learning Process Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stage1[Initialize weights randomly] --> stage2[FORWARD: compute activations]
    stage2 --> stage3[Compute LOSS C]
    stage3 --> stage4[BACKWARD: compute delta for all layers]
    stage4 --> stage5[Compute gradients wrt W and b]
    stage5 --> stage6[UPDATE: W minus eta times grad]
    stage6 --> stage7{Converged?}
    stage7 -->|No| stage2
    stage7 -->|Yes| stage8[Trained network ready]
```
