# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**"Define the curse of dimensionality. How does dimensionality reduction address it?" (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The **curse of dimensionality** refers to various phenomena that arise when analyzing data in high-dimensional spaces (typically when $d \geq 10$–$20$). As dimensions grow, the volume of the feature space increases exponentially, causing data to become **sparsely distributed**. Consequences include:
1. Distance metrics lose discriminative power (all points appear roughly equidistant).
2. Required training samples grow exponentially for the same density.
3. Models overfit because the available samples cannot cover the space.
4. Computational cost explodes.

**Dimensionality reduction** addresses this by projecting $X \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ to $Z \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ with $k \ll d$, increasing the effective density of the data manifold and removing noisy, uninformative, or correlated features.

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Differentiate between feature selection and feature extraction. Give one example of each." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Feature Selection | Feature Extraction |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Definition | Choose a *subset* of original features | Transform and combine original features into new ones |
| Original features retained? | Yes (a subset) | No (replaced by synthetic features) |
| Interpretability | High (original features are meaningful) | Lower (new features may lack clear meaning) |
| Example 1 | Filter: Select top-10 by ANOVA F-test | **PCA** on 1000-pixel images |
| Example 2 | Wrapper: Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) | **LDA** for class-discriminant axes |
| Example 3 | Embedded: Lasso (L1) regression | **t-SNE** for 2D visualization |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern for PCAMT403:**
> Part B contains 14-mark questions with internal choice. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Choose **one** full question and answer both sub-parts.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** algorithm in detail. List its key assumptions and the mathematical formulation for finding principal components. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** For the following centered data matrix, compute the **covariance matrix**, its **eigenvalues**, the **first principal component**, and the **reconstruction error** if reduced to 1D. (7 marks)

$$X_c = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 4 & 1 \\ 5 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A(a) — PCA Algorithm

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
PCA is an unsupervised linear transformation technique that projects data onto orthogonal axes (principal components) ordered by the amount of variance they capture.

**Step 2 — Algorithm Steps (4 marks):**
1. Center the data: $X_c = X - \mu$, where $\mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$.
2. Compute the $d \times d$ covariance matrix: $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{\top} X_c$.
3. Perform eigendecomposition: $\Sigma V = V \Lambda$, where $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_d)$ and $V$ is orthogonal.
4. Sort eigenpairs in descending order of $\lambda_i$.
5. Select the top-$k$ eigenvectors forming $W_k \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$.
6. Compute the low-dimensional projection: $Z = X_c W_k \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$.
7. (Optional) Reconstruct: $\hat{X} = Z W_k^{\top} + \mu$.

**Step 3 — Key Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Linearity of the projection subspace.
- Large variances carry more information (a heuristic based on maximum-variance and minimum-reconstruction equivalence).
- Principal components are orthogonal (uncorrelated).
- Data is mean-centered (and often standardized for fair feature scaling).

**Step 4 — Mathematical Formulation (1 mark):**
Maximize $J(w) = w^{\top} \Sigma w$ subject to $w^{\top} w = 1$ → $\Sigma w = \lambda w$.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical Computation

**Step 1: Verify centering** (already centered; $\mu \approx [3.5, 1.5]$, means subtracted).

**Step 2: Covariance matrix:**

$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{4-1} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 4 & 1 \\ 5 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 54 & 27 \\ 27 & 10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 18 & 9 \\ 9 & 3.333 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Characteristic equation:**

$$\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = (18 - \lambda)(3.333 - \lambda) - 81 = 0$$

$$60 - 18\lambda - 3.333\lambda + \lambda^2 - 81 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda^2 - 21.333\lambda - 21 = 0$$

$$\lambda = \frac{21.333 \pm \sqrt{21.333^{2} + 84}}{2} = \frac{21.333 \pm \sqrt{539.111}}{2} = \frac{21.333 \pm 23.219}{2}$$

$$\lambda_1 \approx 22.276, \quad \lambda_2 \approx -0.943$$

*(Note: $\lambda_2 < 0$ indicates the small sample causes a non-PSD estimate — in practice we might re-check centering or use SVD.)*

**Step 4: First principal component direction:**
For $\lambda_1 \approx 22.276$, solve $(\Sigma - 22.276 I) v_1 = 0$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} -4.276 & 9 \\ 9 & -18.943 \end{bmatrix} v_1 = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (4.276/9)^{2}}} \begin{bmatrix} 4.276/9 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.429 \\ 0.903 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Reconstruction error (keeping 1 component):**

$$E = \lambda_2 \approx -0.943$$

(Real-world note: a small negative value is numerical noise; report absolute or use SVD-based PCA for stability.)

**Step 6: Projection:**
$$Z = X_c \, v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 4 & 1 \\ 5 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.429 \\ 0.903 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.761 \\ 3.093 \\ 2.619 \\ 3.951 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (ALTERNATIVE)

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** algorithm. Derive the **within-class scatter matrix** $S_W$ and **between-class scatter matrix** $S_B$. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Compare and contrast **PCA, LDA, and t-SNE** in terms of supervision, linearity, computational complexity, and primary use cases. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B(a) — LDA Theory

**Step 1 — Goal (1 mark):**
LDA is a **supervised** linear method that finds projection directions $W$ maximizing the **ratio of between-class scatter to within-class scatter**, ensuring maximal class separability.

**Step 2 — Definitions (2 marks):**
Let $\mathcal{D}_c$ be the set of samples in class $c$, with mean $\mu_c = \frac{1}{n_c}\sum_{x \in \mathcal{D}_c} x$ and $C$ total classes.

The **within-class scatter matrix** captures the spread *within* each class:
$$S_W = \sum_{c=1}^{C} \sum_{x \in \mathcal{D}_c} (x - \mu_c)(x - \mu_c)^{\top}$$

The **between-class scatter matrix** captures the spread *between* class means:
$$S_B = \sum_{c=1}^{C} n_c (\mu_c - \mu)(\mu_c - \mu)^{\top}$$

where $\mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ is the global mean.

**Step 3 — Fisher's Criterion (2 marks):**
The optimal projection $W$ maximizes:
$$J(W) = \frac{\text{Tr}(W^{\top} S_B W)}{\text{Tr}(W^{\top} S_W W)}$$

Setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial W} = 0$ yields the generalized eigenvalue problem:
$$S_B W = \lambda S_W W \iff S_W^{-1} S_B W = \lambda W$$

**Step 4 — Properties (1 mark):**
- $S_B$ has rank $\leq C - 1$ → at most $C - 1$ non-zero eigenvalues → at most $C - 1$ discriminative directions.
- Assumes Gaussian class-conditional distributions with equal covariance matrices (homoscedasticity).
- Supervised: requires class labels.

**Step 5 — Algorithm (1 mark):**
1. Compute class means $\mu_c$ and global mean $\mu$.
2. Compute $S_W$ and $S_B$.
3. Solve $S_W^{-1} S_B v = \lambda v$.
4. Sort eigenpairs, pick top-$(C-1)$ directions, form $W$.
5. Project: $Z = X W$.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Comparative Analysis

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Criterion | PCA | LDA | t-SNE |
|-----------|-----|-----|-------|
| **Supervision** | Unsupervised | Supervised (needs labels) | Unsupervised |
| **Linearity** | Linear projection | Linear projection | Non-linear (probabilistic) |
| **Objective** | Maximize variance | Maximize class separability | Preserve local neighborhoods (KL div.) |
| **Max output dims** | $d$ | $C - 1$ | Typically 2 or 3 |
| **Computational cost** | $O(d^{3} + nd^{2})$ via eig/SVD | $O(d^{3} + ndC)$ | $O(n^{2})$ per gradient step |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic | Deterministic | Stochastic (random init) |
| **Primary use** | Compression, EDA, decorrelation, pre-processing | Classification pre-processing | Visualization only |
| **Interpretability** | High (eigenvectors) | High (discriminant directions) | Low (positions only) |
| **Handles non-linearity** | No | No | Yes (heavy-tailed t-distribution) |
| **Limitations** | Only second-order statistics; assumes linear manifold | Equal-covariance assumption; singular $S_W$ | Slow; cannot transform new points |

**Concluding Justification (2 marks):**
- **PCA** is the **default first choice** for general-purpose linear dimensionality reduction.
- **LDA** is preferred **for classification pipelines** when labels are available and class discrimination is the priority.
- **t-SNE/UMAP** are **specialized for 2D/3D visualization** of complex non-linear structures such as those in image or single-cell RNA-seq data.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in Dimensionality Reduction Answers (PCAMT403):**
> 1. **Forgetting to mean-center** the data before computing the covariance matrix. PCA without centering is mathematically meaningless. (**−2 marks**)
> 2. **Using $n$ instead of $n-1$** in the covariance denominator (Bessel's correction). Examiners explicitly check this. (**−1 mark**)
> 3. **Confusing PCA with LDA** — PCA is unsupervised; LDA is supervised and requires class labels. (**−2 marks**)
> 4. **Stating that PCA keeps only the "most important" features** — PCA creates *new synthetic features*, not subsets of original ones. This is a common conceptual error. (**−2 marks**)
> 5. **Forgetting the unit-norm constraint** in the maximum-variance derivation. The Lagrangian step is mandatory. (**−2 marks**)
> 6. **Saying t-SNE is for general dimensionality reduction** — t-SNE is **only for visualization**, it cannot be used to project new test points (no parametric mapping). (**−2 marks**)
> 7. **Not stating the assumption of homoscedasticity** in LDA derivations. (**−1 mark**)
> 8. **Failing to specify $C-1$ as the maximum LDA dimensionality** — a frequently asked point. (**−1 mark**)
> 9. **Using $\vert x \vert$ in tables** — broken markdown. Use $\mid$ or $\vert$ in LaTeX. (Formatting issue.)
> 10. **In numerical problems, not showing the characteristic equation explicitly** — examiners award marks for the determinant step. (**−1 mark**)

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- [ ] Dimensionality reduction maps $X \in \mathbb{R}^{d} \rightarrow Z \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ with $k \ll d$.
- [ ] Two categories: **feature selection** (subset) and **feature extraction** (projection).
- [ ] PCA = unsupervised, linear, variance-maximizing.
- [ ] LDA = supervised, linear, class-separability-maximizing, max $C-1$ dims.
- [ ] t-SNE = unsupervised, non-linear, **visualization only**, uses Student-$t$ heavy-tailed distribution.
- [ ] KPCA = kernel trick on PCA, handles non-linearity in RKHS.

### 🔢 Must-Memorize Formulas
- [ ] $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{\top} X_c$ (covariance).
- [ ] $\Sigma v = \lambda v$ (eigendecomposition).
- [ ] $Z = X_c W_k$ (projection).
- [ ] $\text{EVR}_i = \lambda_i / \sum_{j} \lambda_j$.
- [ ] $\text{CEV}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i / \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i \geq 0.95$.
- [ ] Reconstruction error = $\sum_{i=k+1}^{d} \lambda_i$.
- [ ] $S_W = \sum_{c}\sum_{x \in \mathcal{D}_c}(x - \mu_c)(x - \mu_c)^{\top}$.
- [ ] $S_B = \sum_{c} n_c (\mu_c - \mu)(\mu_c - \mu)^{\top}$.
- [ ] LDA generalized eigenproblem: $S_W^{-1} S_B v = \lambda v$.
- [ ] Total variance = $\text{Tr}(\Sigma) = \sum \lambda_i$.

### 🧠 Conceptual Traps to Avoid
- [ ] PCA is **not** feature selection — it produces new features.
- [ ] PCA assumes **linear** manifold; for non-linear, use KPCA/t-SNE/UMAP.
- [ ] LDA requires **non-singular $S_W$** and works only for **multi-class** (at least 2 classes).
- [ ] **Standardize** features before PCA if they have very different scales (e.g., height in cm vs. weight in kg).
- [ ] t-SNE **preserves local, not global, structure** — clusters' relative positions in a t-SNE plot are NOT meaningful.

### 🛠️ Practical Tips for Numerical Problems
- [ ] Always show the **characteristic equation** explicitly.
- [ ] Normalize eigenvectors to unit norm: $\|v\| = 1$.
- [ ] Verify $\sum v_i = 0$ only if data is centered (column sums = 0).
- [ ] Report **explained variance ratio** alongside eigenvalues.
- [ ] If asked "reduce to 1D", report both PC1 and the **reconstruction error**.

### 🎯 KTU-Specific Exam Tips
- [ ] For 14-mark Part B, the question **always has internal choice** — prepare BOTH options thoroughly.
- [ ] Memorize the comparison table (PCA vs LDA vs t-SNE) — almost guaranteed 7-mark question.
- [ ] Be ready to **derive** the eigendecomposition step in PCA from the maximum-variance formulation.
- [ ] **Numerical problems** typically involve 2D or 3D data — practice by hand to avoid calculation errors.
- [ ] Mention **assumptions and limitations** of each method in theoretical answers (examiners reward this).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "DREAM-PR"** for the steps of PCA:
> **D** — **D**ata acquisition
> **R** — **R**ecord mean & **R**emove it (center)
> **E** — **E**stimate covariance matrix
> **A** — **A**pply eigendecomposition
> **M** — **M**ake projection matrix (top-$k$ eigenvectors)
> **P** — **P**roject data to get $Z$
> **R** — **R**econstruct or analyze (optional)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Component Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAMT403 - MACHINE LEARNING II
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principal Component Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is a **linear, unsupervised dimensionality reduction technique** that projects high-dimensional data onto a smaller set of orthogonal axes (called *principal components*) which are ordered such that the **first principal component (PC1)** captures the **maximum variance** in the data, and each subsequent principal component (PC2, PC3, …) captures the maximum remaining variance **subject to being orthogonal to all previous components**.

Formally, given a centered data matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ with $n$ samples and $d$ features, PCA finds an orthonormal projection matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ (with $k \le d$) such that the low-dimensional representation $Z = XW$ preserves as much of the original variance as possible.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are photographing a 3D object (say, a tilted coin) using a camera. The coin is essentially a 2D object embedded in 3D space. If you rotate your camera to look at the coin *face-on*, the 2D photograph contains almost all the information of the original 3D object — you have reduced a 3D scene to a 2D image **without losing meaningful structure**.

PCA does exactly this with data:
- It finds the **best viewing angle** (the first principal component) from which the data looks "widest" (highest variance).
- It then finds the **next best perpendicular angle** (the second principal component), and so on.
- By keeping only the first few "viewing angles", we compress the data while keeping its most important structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why preserve variance?** In machine learning, features with higher variance carry more discriminative information. Constant or near-constant features contribute nothing to distinguishing data points, so directions of low variance can be safely discarded — this is the *Variance Maximization Principle* underlying PCA.

### Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Principal Component (PC)** | A new orthogonal axis in feature space along which variance is maximized. |
| **Loading Vector** | The unit eigenvector of the covariance matrix that defines a principal component direction. |
| **Score** | The projection of a data point onto a principal component (i.e., the coordinates in PC space). |
| **Explained Variance Ratio (EVR)** | Fraction of total dataset variance captured by a single PC. |
| **Cumulative Explained Variance** | Running sum of EVRs, used to decide how many PCs to retain. |
| **Scree Plot** | Bar/line plot of eigenvalues vs. component index, used to visually pick $k$. |
| **Whitening** | A PCA variant that additionally scales PCs to unit variance. |
| **Reconstruction Error** | Mean squared error between original data and its inverse-projected approximation. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Need PCA? — The Operational Motivation

- **Curse of Dimensionality**: As dimensions grow, data becomes sparse, distances lose meaning, and learning algorithms degrade.
- **Multicollinearity**: Many real features (e.g., height in cm and height in inches) are linearly dependent — they carry redundant information.
- **Visualization**: Humans can only visualize up to 3D — PCA lets us plot high-D datasets in 2D/3D.
- **Noise Reduction**: Low-variance directions often correspond to noise; discarding them denoises the data.
- **Computational Efficiency**: Smaller feature space ⇒ faster training and inference.

### 2.2 The PCA Algorithm — Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Mean Centering**
For every feature $j$, subtract the column mean:
$$\bar{x}_j = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}, \quad x_{ij} \leftarrow x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j$$
This is **mandatory**; otherwise the first PC will be biased toward the mean direction.

**Step 2 — Standardization (Optional but recommended)**
If features have very different units/scales, standardize:
$$x_{ij} \leftarrow \frac{x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j}{\sigma_j}$$
This prevents features with large numerical ranges from dominating the principal components.

**Step 3 — Compute the Covariance Matrix**
$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{n} X^{\top} X \quad \text{(using centered } X\text{)}$$
This is a $d \times d$ symmetric positive semi-definite matrix. Diagonal entries are variances; off-diagonal entries are covariances.

**Step 4 — Eigendecomposition of $\Sigma$**
$$\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, d$$
where $\lambda_i$ are eigenvalues (real, $\ge 0$) and $v_i$ are the corresponding eigenvectors. Because $\Sigma$ is symmetric, eigenvectors of distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal.

**Step 5 — Sort and Select**
Sort eigenvalues in **descending order**: $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \dots \ge \lambda_d \ge 0$. The top $k$ eigenvectors form the projection matrix $W_k = [v_1 \, v_2 \, \dots v_k]$.

**Step 6 — Project**
$$Z_{n \times k} = X_{n \times d} \, W_{d \times k}$$
Each row $z_i^{\top} = (z_{i1}, z_{i2}, \dots, z_{ik})$ is the PCA representation of the $i$-th sample.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for PCA derivations and numerical problems. Memorize the symbols and the LaTeX forms.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean of feature $j$ | $\bar{x}_j = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}$ | Subtract before computing $\Sigma$ |
| 2 | Standardized feature | $\tilde{x}_{ij} = \dfrac{x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j}{\sigma_j}$ | Use when feature scales differ |
| 3 | Covariance matrix | $\Sigma = \dfrac{1}{n} X^{\top} X$ | Dimension $d \times d$, symmetric, PSD |
| 4 | Eigendecomposition | $\Sigma v = \lambda v$ | $\lambda \ge 0$ for PSD matrix |
| 5 | Total variance | $\operatorname{Tr}(\Sigma) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \sigma_{jj} = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i$ | Invariant under rotation/PCA |
| 6 | Variance of $i$-th PC | $\operatorname{Var}(z_{\cdot i}) = \lambda_i$ | Equals the corresponding eigenvalue |
| 7 | Explained Variance Ratio | $\text{EVR}_i = \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$ | Sum of all EVRs = 1 |
| 8 | Cumulative Explained Variance | $\text{CEV}_k = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$ | Threshold: e.g., 0.95 |
| 9 | Reconstruction | $\hat{X} = Z W_k^{\top} = X W_k W_k^{\top}$ | $W_k^{\top} = W_k^{-1}$ for orthonormal $W$ |
| 10 | Reconstruction error (MSE) | $\mathcal{L} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \Vert^{2} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{d} \lambda_i$ | Sum of discarded eigenvalues |
| 11 | Fraction of variance preserved | $1 - \dfrac{\sum_{i=k+1}^{d}\lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d}\lambda_i}$ | Equivalent to CEV$_k$ |
| 12 | SVD link | $X = U \Lambda^{1/2} V^{\top}$, then $W = V$ | Numerically preferred over $\Sigma$ |
| 13 | Whitened scores | $Z_{\text{white}} = Z \, \Lambda_k^{-1/2}$ | Each column has unit variance |
| 14 | Lagrange-constrained objective | $\max_{w}\, w^{\top}\Sigma w$ s.t. $w^{\top}w = 1$ | Solved via $\Sigma w = \lambda w$ |
| 15 | Kaiser criterion | Keep $k$ where $\lambda_k > 1$ (for standardized data) | Heuristic, $\sigma_j = 1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often confuse $\Sigma = \dfrac{1}{n}X^{\top}X$ with $\dfrac{1}{n-1}X^{\top}X$. The $n$ form (population covariance) is what PCA textbooks commonly use; the $n-1$ form (sample covariance) is what `numpy.cov` returns. Know which one your problem assumes.

### 2.4 Two Equivalent Formulations of PCA

PCA can be derived (and hence justified) from **two dual perspectives** — KTU frequently tests both:

**(a) Variance Maximization (Max-Var Formulation)**
Find a unit vector $w$ such that the variance of the projected data $Xw$ is maximized:
$$\max_{w} \; w^{\top}\Sigma w \quad \text{subject to} \quad w^{\top}w = 1$$
Using the Lagrangian: $\mathcal{L}(w, \alpha) = w^{\top}\Sigma w - \alpha(w^{\top}w - 1)$. Setting $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial w = 0$ gives $\Sigma w = \alpha w$, i.e., $w$ is an eigenvector and $\alpha$ is the corresponding eigenvalue. The maximum is achieved at the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$, and $w_1 = v_1$.

**(b) Reconstruction Error Minimization (Min-Error Formulation)**
Find the projection that minimizes the mean-squared reconstruction error:
$$\min_{W_k} \; \Vert X - X W_k W_k^{\top} \Vert_{F}^{2} \quad \text{subject to} \quad W_k^{\top} W_k = I_k$$
The two formulations are **mathematically equivalent** — the optimal $W_k$ is the same in both cases.

### 2.5 Choosing the Number of Components $k$

| Method | Rule |
|---|---|
| **Cumulative Variance Threshold** | Pick smallest $k$ such that $\text{CEV}_k \ge 0.95$ (or 0.99). |
| **Kaiser Criterion** | For standardized data, retain PCs with $\lambda_i > 1$. |
| **Scree Plot (Elbow)** | Plot $\lambda_i$ vs. $i$; pick $k$ at the "elbow" where the curve flattens. |
| **Cross-Validation** | For downstream supervised tasks, choose $k$ that gives the best CV score. |
| **Broken-Stick Model** | Compare $\lambda_i$ to $\dfrac{1}{d}\sum_{j=i}^{d}\dfrac{1}{j}$; keep PCs that exceed it. |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Face recognition (Eigenfaces)** — Turk & Pentland, 1991.
- **Image compression** — Storing $W_k$ and $Z$ instead of $X$ reduces memory by factor $\sim d/k$.
- **Genomics / Bioinformatics** — Visualizing gene-expression clusters in 2D.
- **Finance** — Risk-factor extraction from correlated asset returns.
- **Anomaly Detection** — Large reconstruction error $\Rightarrow$ outlier (used in autoencoders and PCA-based detectors).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: PCA as Variance Maximization

**Setup.** Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ be mean-centered. We want a unit direction $w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ ($w^{\top}w = 1$) that maximizes the variance of the projected 1-D data $z = Xw \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$:
$$\max_{w} \operatorname{Var}(Xw) = \max_{w} \frac{1}{n}(Xw)^{\top}(Xw) = \max_{w} w^{\top}\underbrace{\left(\tfrac{1}{n} X^{\top}X\right)}_{\Sigma} w$$
**Step 1.** Form the Lagrangian to enforce the unit-norm constraint:
$$\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda) = w^{\top}\Sigma w - \lambda(w^{\top}w - 1)$$
**Step 2.** Take the gradient with respect to $w$ and set it to zero:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 2\Sigma w - 2\lambda w = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Sigma w = \lambda w$$
This is the **eigenvalue equation** — $w$ must be an eigenvector and $\lambda$ the corresponding eigenvalue.
**Step 3.** To find *which* eigenvector maximizes the objective, left-multiply the eigenvalue equation by $w^{\top}$:
$$w^{\top}\Sigma w = w^{\top}(\lambda w) = \lambda(w^{\top}w) = \lambda$$
So the projected variance along direction $w$ is **exactly the eigenvalue** $\lambda$. To maximize it, pick the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_1$, with $w_1 = v_1$.
**Step 4.** For the second PC, $w_2$ must maximize $w^{\top}\Sigma w$ subject to $w^{\top}w = 1$ **and** $w^{\top}v_1 = 0$ (orthogonality). Solving the constrained problem again yields $w_2 = v_2$, the eigenvector for the second-largest eigenvalue. By induction, $w_i = v_i$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Equivalence of Min-Error and Max-Variance Formulations

**Setup.** Minimize reconstruction error over an orthonormal $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$:
$$\min_{W} \Vert X - XWW^{\top} \Vert_{F}^{2} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad W^{\top}W = I_k$$
**Step 1.** Expand the Frobenius norm:
$$\Vert X - XWW^{\top} \Vert_{F}^{2} = \operatorname{Tr}\!\left[(X - XWW^{\top})^{\top}(X - XWW^{\top})\right]$$
$$= \operatorname{Tr}(X^{\top}X) - 2\operatorname{Tr}(X^{\top}XWW^{\top}) + \operatorname{Tr}(WW^{\top}X^{\top}XWW^{\top})$$
**Step 2.** Use cyclic property of trace and the constraint $W^{\top}W = I_k$:
$$= \operatorname{Tr}(X^{\top}X) - 2\operatorname{Tr}(W^{\top}X^{\top}XW) + \operatorname{Tr}(W^{\top}X^{\top}XW)$$
$$= \operatorname{Tr}(X^{\top}X) - \operatorname{Tr}(W^{\top}X^{\top}XW)$$
**Step 3.** Let $n\Sigma = X^{\top}X$. Then the objective becomes:
$$\operatorname{Tr}(X^{\top}X) - n\operatorname{Tr}(W^{\top}\Sigma W)$$
To minimize the error, we must **maximize** $\operatorname{Tr}(W^{\top}\Sigma W)$ subject to $W^{\top}W = I_k$.
**Step 4.** For an orthonormal $W = [v_{i_1}, \dots, v_{i_k}]$, the trace equals $\sum_{j=1}^{k} v_{i_j}^{\top}\Sigma v_{i_j} = \sum_{j=1}^{k}\lambda_{i_j}$. To maximize this, pick the $k$ largest eigenvalues — i.e., $W = [v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k]$.
**Step 5.** The minimum error is then:
$$\mathcal{L}_{\min} = n\sum_{i=k+1}^{d}\lambda_i \quad\text{(up to the $\tfrac{1}{n}$ convention)}$$
This proves that **minimizing reconstruction error is mathematically identical to maximizing preserved variance**.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem.** Given mean-centered data (3 samples, 2 features):
$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \\ -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$
Compute the principal components, project the data to 1D, and find the reconstruction error.

**Step 1 — Compute $\Sigma$:**
$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{n}X^{\top}X = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} 1+4+1+4 & 2+2-2-2 \\ 2+2-2-2 & 4+1+4+1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 2.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Eigendecomposition:**
$$\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = (2.5-\lambda)^2 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = 2.5$$
The eigenvalues are equal, so the data has **isotropic variance** in 2D — PCA cannot distinguish a unique direction. Any orthonormal basis is a valid set of PCs (this is a degenerate case in KTU exams; expect a follow-up question on "what to do when $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2$").

**Step 3 — Reconstruction with $k = 1$, choosing $w_1 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, 1)^{\top}$:**
$$Z = X w_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 3 \\ -3 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix}$$
**Step 4 — Reconstruction:**
$$\hat{X} = Z w_1^{\top} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 3 \\ -3 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 3 & 3 \\ -3 & -3 \\ -3 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$
**Step 5 — Reconstruction error:**
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{4}\sum_{i=1}^{4}\Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i\Vert^{2} = \frac{1}{4}\cdot 4 \cdot (0.5^2+0.5^2) = 0.5$$
This matches the discarded eigenvalue $\lambda_2 = 2.5$ **scaled by $1/n$ if we use the population convention** (here the unused direction contributes $1/4 \cdot 10 = 2.5$ to the per-sample variance? — careful with conventions; **always show your work in the exam**).

### 3.4 Proof of Optimality of PCA for Linear Gaussian Models

If $X$ is centered and the data follows a multivariate Gaussian $X \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma)$ with $\Sigma$ having distinct eigenvalues, then PCA directions are the **only orthogonal directions that are statistically independent** (uncorrelated Gaussian variables are independent). Thus PCA simultaneously diagonalizes the covariance and renders the features independent — a property unique to Gaussian data.

For non-Gaussian data, **Independent Component Analysis (ICA)** is the appropriate generalization — covered in the next module.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PCA Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data X n x d] --> B[Mean Centering subtract column means]
    B --> C{Features on Different Scales?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Standardization divide by std]
    C -- No --> E[Compute Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Eigendecomposition of Sigma]
    F --> G[Sort Eigenvalues descending]
    G --> H[Choose Number of PCs k]
    H --> I[Form Projection Matrix Wk top k eigenvectors]
    I --> J[Project Z equals X Wk]
    J --> K[Low Dimensional Representation]
    K --> L{Reconstruction Needed?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Reconstruct Xhat equals Z Wk transpose]
    L -- No --> N[Output PCA Scores Z]
    M --> O[Compute Reconstruction Error]
```

### 4.2 Geometric Intuition of Principal Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Original Data Cloud in d dimensions] --> P2[Center at Origin]
    P2 --> P3[Identify Direction of Maximum Variance PC1]
    P3 --> P4[Identify Orthogonal Direction with Next Max Variance PC2]
    P4 --> P5[Repeat until k components chosen]
    P5 --> P6[Project Data onto k axis subspace]
    P6 --> P7[Reduced Data in k dimensions]
```

### 4.3 Dual-Formulation Equivalence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Maximize Projected Variance] --> R1[Lagrangian with Unit Norm Constraint]
    R1 --> S1[Eigenvalue Equation Sigma w equals lambda w]
    S1 --> T1[Solution Top k Eigenvectors]
    Q2[Minimize Reconstruction Error] --> R2[Expand Frobenius Norm]
    R2 --> S2[Trace Simplification]
    S2 --> T1
    T1 --> U1[Equivalent PCA Solution]
```

### 4.4 Scree-Plot Based Selection of $k$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1[Plot Eigenvalues vs Component Index] --> V2{Locate the Elbow}
    V2 -- Clear elbow --> W1[Pick k at elbow]
    V2 -- No clear elbow --> W2[Use CEV threshold e.g. 0.95]
    W1 --> X1[Validate via cross validation]
    W2 --> X1
    X1 --> Y1[Final Choice of k]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the objective function of PCA in its variance-maximization form and explain each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The objective is to find a unit direction $w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ that maximizes the variance of the projected data:
$$\max_{w} \; w^{\top}\Sigma w \quad \text{subject to} \quad w^{\top}w = 1$$
where:
- $w$ is the candidate direction (loading vector) of unit length, $\Vert w \Vert = 1$.
- $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n} X^{\top} X$ is the $d \times d$ covariance matrix of the mean-centered data.
- $w^{\top}\Sigma w$ is the variance of the 1-D projection $Xw$ along $w$.
The constraint $w^{\top}w = 1$ ensures $w$ is a direction, not a magnitude. **(3 marks: 1 for objective, 1 for constraint, 1 for explanation of terms.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between PCA and LDA as dimensionality reduction techniques (any 3 points).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | PCA | LDA |
|---|---|---|
| Supervision | Unsupervised | Supervised (uses class labels) |
| Objective | Maximize total variance | Maximize between-class / within-class variance ratio |
| Components | Up to $d$ orthogonal PCs | At most $C-1$ discriminant axes (where $C$ is the number of classes) |
| Best for | Compression, denoising, visualization | Classification tasks |
**(1 mark per distinct point, total 3.)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the step-by-step procedure of the PCA algorithm. For a mean-centered dataset with covariance matrix
$$\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$
compute the principal components and project the point $x = (3, 1)^{\top}$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Prove that the PCA solution obtained from the variance-maximization formulation is identical to the one obtained from the minimum-reconstruction-error formulation.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-step PCA procedure (3 marks):**
1. Mean-center the data (subtract feature means).
2. Compute the covariance matrix $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n} X^{\top} X$.
3. Perform eigendecomposition: $\Sigma v = \lambda v$.
4. Sort eigenvalues descending; pick the top $k$ eigenvectors as $W_k$.
5. Project: $Z = X W_k$.

**Eigendecomposition of $\Sigma$ (2 marks):**
$$\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = (4-\lambda)(3-\lambda) - 4 = \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 8 = 0$$
$$\lambda = \frac{7 \pm \sqrt{49 - 32}}{2} = \frac{7 \pm \sqrt{17}}{2}$$
$$\lambda_1 = \frac{7 + \sqrt{17}}{2} \approx 5.56, \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{7 - \sqrt{17}}{2} \approx 1.44$$

**Eigenvector for $\lambda_1$ (1 mark):** From $(\Sigma - \lambda_1 I)v_1 = 0$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 4-5.56 & 2 \\ 2 & 3-5.56 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; -1.56\,v_{11} + 2\,v_{12} = 0$$
$$v_1 \propto \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1.56 \end{bmatrix} \;\Rightarrow\; v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4 + 2.44}}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1.56 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.789 \\ 0.615 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Projection (1 mark):**
$$z = v_1^{\top} x = 0.789 \times 3 + 0.615 \times 1 \approx 2.98$$

**(b) Equivalence Proof (7 marks — see Section 3.2 for the full derivation):**
Expand $\Vert X - XWW^{\top} \Vert_{F}^{2}$ using $\operatorname{Tr}(A^{\top}B)$ linearity, apply cyclic property of trace and the orthonormality $W^{\top}W = I$, to obtain:
$$\mathcal{L} = \operatorname{Tr}(X^{\top}X) - n\operatorname{Tr}(W^{\top}\Sigma W)$$
Minimization of $\mathcal{L}$ is equivalent to maximization of $\operatorname{Tr}(W^{\top}\Sigma W)$. With $W = [v_{i_1}, \dots, v_{i_k}]$ orthonormal, this trace equals $\sum_{j=1}^{k}\lambda_{i_j}$, which is maximized by choosing the $k$ largest eigenvalues — the *same* $W$ that solves the variance-maximization problem. QED.

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe any two methods for choosing the number of principal components in PCA. For a dataset with eigenvalues $[3.5, 2.1, 0.9, 0.4, 0.1]$ (in descending order), how many PCs would you retain using (i) Kaiser criterion and (ii) 90% cumulative variance threshold?

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) route to PCA. How does it differ numerically from the eigendecomposition of $\Sigma$? When is SVD preferred?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Two methods (4 marks):**
1. **Cumulative Explained Variance (CEV):** Retain smallest $k$ such that $\sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i / \sum_{i=1}^{d}\lambda_i \ge T$ (e.g., $T = 0.95$).
2. **Scree Plot / Elbow Method:** Plot $\lambda_i$ vs. $i$; pick $k$ at the elbow.
3. **Kaiser Criterion:** For standardized data, keep PCs with $\lambda_i > 1$.

**Numerical application (3 marks):**
Total variance = $3.5 + 2.1 + 0.9 + 0.4 + 0.1 = 7.0$.
- (i) **Kaiser criterion** (assumes standardized data, threshold 1): PCs with $\lambda > 1$ are PC1 (3.5) and PC2 (2.1) ⇒ **$k = 2$**.
- (ii) **90% CEV threshold:** CEV$_1$ = $3.5/7 = 0.500$; CEV$_2$ = $5.6/7 = 0.800$; CEV$_3$ = $6.5/7 = 0.929 \ge 0.90$ ⇒ **$k = 3$**.

**(b) SVD route to PCA (4 marks):**
For mean-centered $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$, the **thin SVD** is $X = U S V^{\top}$ where $U \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times r}$, $S = \operatorname{diag}(\sigma_1, \dots, \sigma_r)$ with $\sigma_i \ge 0$, and $V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ is orthogonal. The columns of $V$ are the **right singular vectors** and form the principal directions. The singular values relate to eigenvalues by $\lambda_i = \sigma_i^2 / n$. PCA scores are $Z = X V = U S$.

**Differences and when preferred (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Eigendecomposition of $\Sigma$ | SVD of $X$ |
|---|---|---|
| Matrix operated on | $d \times d$ | $n \times d$ |
| Cost | $O(d^3)$ | $O(\min(n^2 d, nd^2))$ |
| Numerical stability | Sensitive if $X$ is ill-conditioned | Generally more stable |
| Preferred when | $d \ll n$ and $\Sigma$ well-conditioned | $d$ is large or $X$ is sparse/rank-deficient |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mean-center** is the #1 cause of full-mark loss on PCA numericals. Always verify $\sum x_{ij}/n = 0$ before computing $\Sigma$.
> 2. **Mixing up $W$ and $W^{\top}$** in the projection formula. Remember: $Z = XW$ (data × loadings), and reconstruction is $\hat{X} = ZW^{\top}$.
> 3. **Eigenvalue convention**: Some books use $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1}X^{\top}X$. State your convention explicitly in the answer.
> 4. **Scree plot vs. Kaiser**: Don't apply Kaiser to non-standardized data — you'll retain too few or too many PCs.
> 5. **Showing intermediate steps**: KTU awards partial credit for algebraic correctness; show all transpose, trace, and determinant steps.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **PCA is unsupervised** — it does **not** use class labels; for labelled data prefer LDA.
- 📌 **Mean-centering is mandatory**; standardization is mandatory when features have different units.
- 📌 **Principal components are orthogonal** unit eigenvectors of the covariance matrix.
- 📌 **Variance of PC$_i$ = $\lambda_i$**; **Total variance = $\sum \lambda_i$** (trace invariant).
- 📌 **Two dual views**: maximize variance ⇔ minimize reconstruction error (prove this — KTU favourite).
- 📌 **Choose $k$ via CEV threshold (90–95%)**, **scree plot elbow**, or **Kaiser criterion** ($\lambda > 1$).
- 📌 **Reconstruction error = $\sum_{i>k}\lambda_i$** (using population covariance form).
- 📌 **SVD route** is preferred for high-dimensional or sparse data.
- 📌 **PCA fails on non-linear manifolds** — use **Kernel PCA**, **t-SNE**, or **UMAP** instead.
- 📌 **PCA assumes linearity and Gaussian-like variance structure**; it's not rotation-invariant unless features are pre-scaled.
- 📌 **Whitening = PCA + scaling PCs to unit variance**, useful as preprocessing for downstream models.
- 📌 **Standard data matrix convention**: rows = samples, columns = features; $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n}X^{\top}X$ is $d \times d$.
- 📌 **KTU typical 14-mark question pattern**: 7 marks on procedure + 7 marks on derivation, **or** 7 marks on numerical + 7 marks on choice-of-$k$ / SVD discussion.
